He seems to find joy and glee in rooting out academic dishonesty. Collaboration on projects (which is encouraged on the syllabus) is treated like a life sentence. Take 230 with anyone else.

For some reason, this was my least favorite 200-level class. I enjoyed 220 more than this. Honestly, I had no idea what was going on at the end. Chill guy, but I'm not a big fan of how the course was organized. Felt like I was constantly sprinting, and the topics were of very little interest to me.

I found the subject boring in general, but the way the lectures were done did not help. He is a good guy and it seems like he tries. I watched the lectures they had little relation to the homework and weekly assignments. I'm a senior now and this is still the most poorly taught class I have taken.

I think Joe Chiu gets more hate then is deserved. Yes his slides and lectures are at times bad, and the general course structure can be all over the place, but it wasn't terrible. Projects ranged from easy to difficult and the exams were almost identical to the weekly quizzes (although sort of an unusual format). Language barrier can be difficult.
Horrible class, I hate CS 230. and I am very disapointed in the way this class is set up and taught. thanks for nothing

Great and very caring professor. CS230 is set up with so much care. You can attend lectures, watch them through Zoom in real time, watch the recordings, or watch his pre-recorded videos. His analogies are great and I've learned the most through his class! His teaching style really supports good coding practice and gives you a strong knowledge base.
I hated CS 230. Horrible class.

I know there is a lot of debate on what the hardest 200 level at UMass CS is but I honestly hate CS 230. I think Joe Chiu is funny and he tries, but the class is horribly set up. The asynchronous work just is set up horribly along with weekly quizzes and worksheets, then the very poorly instructed projects and exams.
Great Class, good teacher. Gets a lot of hate but if you actually show up to class and put in the effort it is an easy A. 4/5 difficulty for a 200 level but if it was a 400 level it would be 2/5.
Bad lectures where Joe often just rambles. Projects aren't good either.

Marius cares about his students' learning, puts in effort to make sure his projects/expectations are clear, and is very approachable. Joe Chiu cannot do any of these things. CS230 is a chaotic mess of unclear, fuzzy instructions with equally chaotic grading criteria. Projects are bad and you don't learn much. Worst 200-level I've taken.

guys useless. he designed the class to be totally doable just from his lecture slides, but then his lecture slides are just a bunch of random pictures and diagrams. specifically the cache lesson makes absolutely 0 sense. 230 is super easy though, projects are light. can fail everything else and still pass. overall kind of annoying class
He is a terrible speaker and very unclear about assignments, really impossible to listen to.
I love this guy

Overall, I did like Joe has a professor. Like many others have said though, he does have a thick accent that doesn't incline me to take the class again, but he was extremely caring and nice, making jokes throughout class and leaving some time for questions even bringing candy for when the class is over. Great professor, accent is not his fault.
I did not attend lectures. Joe builds the entire course so that you have the option to take it asynchronously, and most students take advantage of that.

Pre-recorded lectures strayed very far from quiz questions and exam content. In-person lectures were boring and not very helpful. Often left class feeling just as confused as I went in. The only learning I actually did was through the projects. Worst instructor I've had at UMass so far imo.

Joe is super sweet, and really wants his students to do well. CS230 is quite easy at this point, so much so that the lectures aren't really needed, but I still found them useful, especially the class Kahoot reviews at the end of lecture. Make sure to have a good understanding of C going in, as their isn't much review.

Attendance is optional but weekly quizzes can be difficult if you don't attend, keep in mind he does have heavy accent. Projects are biweekly and I found them to be quite easy with one or two office hour visits. Exams are only 30% of your grade so it easy to do well in class, however the exams are mostly multiple choice thus no partial credit

Firstly, he has a thick accent that makes him hard to understand. Additionally, the recorded videos on Moodle are 480p and lack most info present on the slides, info that's also tested. Lastly, exams are graded out of 40 points, so slight mistakes compound into significant underperformances at the class level. Not the worst, but avoid if possible.
This class has too much workload for a 1 credit class, its unacceptable. its almost like 220 but less workload

The lectures were quite boring, but it's easy enough to get by with just his YouTube videos and the slides. 230's content is pretty foundational but honestly not very interesting. Using C can be very annoying, and the projects are substantially more challenging than 187. Exams and quizzes weren't bad, but make sure to be careful on the worksheets.

Walked into class day 1 and had a horrible impression: plagerism policies seemed way too strict, very thick accent, and RateMyProf reviews were not very positive. Nearing the end of the semester, I couldn't say enough good things. If you attend his lectures, follow his directions carefully for graded assignments, and put the work, you will succeed.
Joe has got to go.
Just overall poor. Boring lectures, unclear projects, etc.

Lectures arn't even worth going to, just study the material on your own and it would be better. I also had him for CS187 and remember him yelling at me when I went to his office hours to ask for help on a project. Not a pleasurable experience taking any of his classes.

He was one of 3. Provides entertaining lectures that I found the most engaging. Often, he would simplify ideas well. I preferred him to Elliot Moss, although he sometimes simplifies too much, to the detriment of learning. Overall good, but he seems disorganized, which could be catastrophic in a higher level course, so I wouldn't take him again.

187 with Joe is one of the worst classes I have ever taken. In lectures, he is not motivated or good at teaching. His pronunciation of some words made this class even harder for me. It took me a couple of classes to understand what word he was talking about. Also, the projects were super hard compared to what he thought during the class.

He is absolutely one of the most unforgiving and uncooperative professors I have had the displeasure of having in my lifetime.

Going to his lectures are the largest waste of time. Even if you could understand the words coming out of his mouth, his methods of explanation and lecture are horrible.

He NEVER gives extensions. Period. 0 forgiveness.

Professor Joe was honestly one of the worst professors I have ever had. His lectures don't offer any help with the difficult exams and overwhelming projects. And anytime you ask for help he comes off as rude and makes you feel stupid for asking any questions. He only knows how to teach people who already know how to code. This class was the worst.

Oftentimes very difficult to understand. I did not find his lectures to be particularly useful or interesting after doing the Zybook, which does a much better job of explaining concepts. He is fairly responsive on Campuswire, but sometimes his responses do not help much with your question.

I grew up in a country where Chinese was spoken. You would think I'd get used to hearing a Chinese accent by now but I still have trouble understanding what he says. He's a nice guy and the topics in this class are interesting, but he's not a great teacher.

If you are not a CS major, do not take this class because the professor only knows how to speak to CS majors, who already know the material, which messes up the grading curve. The class moves incredibly fast so much that the dep't is getting rid of this class next semester. Example problems are NOTHING like the exam problems.
Lectures are so boring I can't even pay attention (even recorded ones on moodle). I can't really tell if we are learning anything either?
5 words: a lazy CICS faculty member.

For my year, Joe decided to screw everyone up and redo the course content that was not reviewed by the board. The content was not something that could be covered in one semester. Additionally, Joe, TAs and the student volunteers were not on the same page and would give confusing answers to literally everything. Different answr for the same question

The Labs with the right lab group are manageable, while the Zybooks will carry you through the class. Joe's a very caring and honest guy, he brings candy to lectures, tries to answer questions quickly, and works hard to make sure everyone can pass. His accent can be a bit hard to understand at first but is easy to get used to.

230 under Joe is a class that is just poorly structured with an incompetent teacher. Joe makes no effort to teach his students, his projects have unclear documentation, and he was even called out for not running his classes well. If you can, just take 230 with either Marc Liberatore or Tim Richards. They do a better job running classes.

Joe is a prime example of someone who recently began his villain arc. He doesn't listen to students or care about them anymore, he's being super lazy when it comes to running a class, and his Piazza posts fail to clarify information or fill important knowledge gaps. Good thing he's going to be on sabbatical for the fall of 2022.

Solid professor who does a good job lecturing and explaining the content to students. Attendance isn't mandatory but kahoots and in-class coding walkthroughs help a lot. Projects are solid difficulty but definitely doable.
Joe is solid, a nice guy who cares about his students and very helpful in office hours. The course material is not too difficult.

Extremely difficult for a 1 credit class. Requires 10+ hours per week. The recorded videos are insufficient and you will need to teach yourself C and EdLab. Don't be fooled by the simplicity in the first few weeks - it gets VERY hard. Joe is disorganized and never answers questions well. Had to withdraw for mental health.
Good professor, answers your questions well

Joes lectures were never really helpful. Most of the time it would just be explaining concepts through zybooks that's you can easily just read yourself. In addition, I went to officer hours one time and asked how something was supposed to work and he ended up just yelling at me for not knowing how it should work. I never went again.

This course section taught by Joe is down right awful. I thought Joe's teaching style for 121 was mediocre, but his 187 lectures were so bad I went to the first 2 and then NEVER AGAIN. The course is run at an unrelenting pace so you're always doing the projects back to back... it's a truly miserable experience. Beware taking 187 with Joe!

Watching Joe Chiu's recorded lectures for 198c is an absolute blast. He uses a Snorlax stuffed animal to pose clarifying question, and ends every lecture with "(lecture topic) isss awesome" and then a transition. I love his clear passion for teaching and it makes all the difference in keeping students engaged.

He doesn't know the material and doesn't know how to explain it. No effort is put for students to succeed. Isn't helpful in his responses when you ask him or reach out to him. UCA's are incompetent and the TA's as well. The whole structure of the class needs some change.
Joe Chiu is a mixed bag. He seems like a nice person, but his CS230 class has the worst structure when compared to the other instructors who taught it.

I think Joe is a nice guy but 230 under him just has a horrible structure, basically just an online course and you only have to show up for the exams. Projects are cool but stressful because Joe's responses on piazza are unclear and not helpful. Exam is just reused quiz questions too, very lazy.

Joe Chiu must quit his job as not only did he get a bad name from students for his poor management of 230 in-person, but after the switch to in-person learning, he’s also a perfect example of a professor who has zero respect towards students with disabilities. Good thing he's on leave for the fall of 2022.

Joe Chiu used to be the sweetest professor during COVID, but his recent inability to take criticism after the switch from online to in-person learning and the lack of physical in-person lectures really darkens the respect I have for him as a professor. It sucks because he was very nice and helpful when he co-taught 220 with Marius in spring 2021.
Very poor course management alongside lack of physical lectures should make 230 a hard pass if Joe teaches it. Take 230 with Tim if he’s teaching.

One lecture a week, and it was solely for Kahoot/worksheets; no content. Discussions were just us sitting in silence in a room taking ONLINE quizzes with questions we've never seen. Projects were marred by project quizzes that were 40% of our project grade. The final exam was just the removed quiz questions; average was a 59. Terribly run course.

Does not provide specificity with how he wants work done. Unnecessarily rigid and a poor explainer. Taking his class means relying on zyBooks in order to learn anything, and his Kahoots do absolutely nothing for anybody. I've never been so dissatisfied with a teacher as I have with Chiu, and I would never recommend him to anybody. You can do better

The first 1/3 of 121 is pretty easy with Joe. After that, you will need to put more effort into the class to get good grades. He uses kahoots to quiz students on coding problems, which is kind of hit or miss. He is hard to understand sometimes. If youre struggling in 121 go to his office hours!! He is rlly helpful outside of class.

I believe that Joe is genuinely a nice professor, but he's being super lazy!!! He is reusing old video lectures, has converted one of our 2 lecture sessions to an office hour WHICH SOLVES NOTHING, doesn't even teach in the other lecture, and has us take way too many quizzes each week (project, weekly, lesson). Has a minimal curve to the grade.

Fantastic projects that truly illustrate the essence of the course to the student and completion ensures good fundamental understanding and intuition for similar material (more advanced). But pushing more on tests these days, early morning labs and quizzes, projects given over a short timeline which is not very conducive all things considered.
He is a super caring professor, but the class set up sucks. You have to watch recorded lectures then during lectures/discussions take quizzes or graded worksheets. Tough class overall.

Instead of lecturing, he uses old and outdated lecture videos that are either in low quality (480p) or riddled with mistakes (which he never address). Moreover, he couldn't even speak English properly, therefore his videos are extremely hard to follow. He gets defensive and would clap bank at students who express doubts or question his teaching.

CS230 is about making projects. Projects need documentation (guide of what you need to do) so he can grade it. If not, you will create arbitrary projects yourself. Joe's doc are often missing things. In short, he deducts your score based on what he hides/forget to add to doc.
Very bad professor. Don't take him.
Dead from the class. My GPA will not recover from this blow! The class is not hard, but he did not teach anything throughout the semester.

What a hilarious prof. Tries to cover up for his incompetency with a soft animals like people will not find out he's not doing anything and reusing recorded videos from the summer. Truly a letdown for Robert and Donna Manning College Of Information and Computer Sciences.

The fact that a "flipped classroom model" was implemented after this class was taught online is not lost on me. It seems like he was able to cut down how much he actually contributes to the course by having students learn on their own and pretending that a single synchronous meeting in which you are quizzed is called teaching. I call it lazy.

Great professor; really knowledgeable about the material, responds quickly to questions on Piazza, wants everyone to succeed, and actively asks for our feedback. The only reason I didn't give 5 is that his accent and assignments can be hard to understand, but it's not too much of a problem, I've gotten better at understanding him.

Professor Joe is a great professor. The projects are hard, but they will make you learn. The format of the class works well for but I can see how others could struggle. He cares about his students and wants us to succeed.
Joe is a terrible professor and this class has been awful.

Great professor. Took his 220 with Marius Minea. Lighthearted, cute, a bit cringe, good slides and videos. His quiz is often confusing due to the accent. Sometimes he may sound ironic, but actually it's him being chilled.

Level of difficulty is 4 on the basis of 198c being a 1 credit class, but felt like 3 credit class workload. I've had Prof. Chiu for 198c during the summer along with 220 in spring 2020, and it is safe to say he is the most passionate and caring educator in my entire scholastic career. He explains complex concepts in a very easy to understand way.

Joe is quite a passionate professor. You can tell that he puts a lot of effort into his lectures, and he answers Piazza almost instantly, even when it's late at night. The only one down side of Joe is that his accent is a bit hard to understand, but if you can get used to it he is quite a good professor.

Combo of lesson vids w live lectures was perfect. KAHOOT was the best idea ever. Live lectures were very well organized: summary -> Kahoot (with thorough explanations) -> class activities. Very helpful during OH. Class is one of the best and one of the hardest ever. Does not repeat stuff, but rather explains material in new ways.
Joe is a great professor. Very caring, tries really hard to make the course fair for us. Always cleared up confusion, and answered questions extremely fast. Could not recommend enough.

Joe is a very nice professor. However, beware of this class. CS220 is awful and very annoying. The average on the weekly quiz is an F. And the average on the final exam was 19/65 (also an F). This class is much worse than CS187.

Joe is one of the kindest professors in the CS department. He really truly cares about his students and he wants you to succeed. He will do his absolute best to help you. The classes can be hard, but none of that falls on Joe imo. Great prof.

Yeah, definitely had to work hard to get my grade in the course. Concepts aren't bad, but C in general isn't the easiest to pick up for me to be honest. Joe is nice and caring. You can succeed, but you need to put in the work and stay on top of things.

Joe obviously cares about his students and wants to help them pass. It seems like the course guidelines the CS department gives him are rigid, though he tries to be as helpful as he can. The switch to remote learning was bumpy and he made a few mistakes, but he obviously had the students' best interests at heart.

Joe clearly knows the subject well but he is not suited to be a professor, at least at this level. His accent is extremely hard to understand, and the slides and posts that he writes are riddled with mistakes. I learned almost everything in this class from the projects, which are a lot tougher than the CS121 projects.

JOE IS AMAZING and cares about his students. One time he stuck around for AN HOUR after class ended to give me help on a challenge (I ended up getting it). He brings candy too to class! NOTE: 230 starts off with an intense worked, then tapers off by spring break (or mid semester). First project took 20h, last took 5h #covid19semester
I think the guy is really smart but it's impossible to understand him because of his thick asian accent. I'm trying to learn and I can't understand him. Take another comsci professor if possible.

Joe Chiu is one of the worst professors Ive ever had. Hes not helpful at all and those who say he is most likely have prior knowledge about C programming or are overconfident in their abilities. He isnt lenient with most things like due dates and hes very cynical towards you when you try to ask simple questions. Dont take his class.

He has the absolute worst English and doesn't deserve to teach this course. There are SO MANY mistakes in anything he writes. He wrote one new project for our semester and it had the WORST documentation ever. He wrote a midterm with an unholy amount of mistakes and ambiguities. His accent is bad. One has to scout Piazza for everything.

While Joe himself is pretty good at explaining, his accent makes him hard to understand in lectures. The CS department, made this the hardest class you can take, putting in measures to intentionally lower your grade, like trick questions in exams, graded iclicker answers, and lab assignments with varying due dates. I'd still recommend Joe though.

The only downfall of Joe is his English ability, especially when he writes exams. I have found him to be really caring in office hours, and he is a fairly good professor in my experience. Just be wary of what he says during exams in terms of corrections; many times his questions are poorly worded, so ask him directly, NOT the TA's.

Joe is a good professor. Yes, his accent is kinda heavy but you can still clearly understand him. He is also very helpful in office hours. 187 is one of the project heavy class so be ready to do work in it.

Joes is so kind and he is so helpful at office hours. He always wants to make sure that students are understanding material. Yes, his accent is a little difficult to understand but just ask and he can rephrase what he is saying. Also, don't buy into the hype of 187. Yes it is difficult, but treat it as another hard class to succeed in!
Joe is literally a god. If you don't take Joe for 187 you are betraying god himself.
Joe is literally a god. If you don't take Joe for 187 you are betraying god himself.
I made the mistake of choosing the other professor for this class. Switched lectures to Joe's after dropping in once. Best decision I've ever made.

Horrible accent that makes understanding him hard. Lousy at communicating. Teaches purely from the slides. Offers nothing beyond that I can get a Coursera or edX now. I can't believe I'm paying this much in tuition and have such poor quality professor.

I took CS 187 with him in the fall and I wasn't too much of a fan. However, taking 220 in the spring, he made major improvements as a lecturer and overall is extremely caring and nice to his students. He is a great professor and I hope to have him in CS 230!
Awesome and funny teachers. It is quite hard to listen to what he said at first because his English accent is not so good but the lecture is still amazing.
Best instructor ever despite his English accent is a little bit hard to hear but you will get used to it. He is always passionate about the lecture materials, and he is also funny.
his accent is a little hard to hear but the slide is very helpful and he eager to explain everything you ask. His lecture is very inspirational and hilarious.
Although his accent is a large barrier, if you are focused in class, you can pass the class effortless. If you have any question after class, he's always available.

A gem of an instructor. I have had him for two classes, TA for CS187 and now CS220, will sign up for his CS230 section as well! Very accessible and doesn't have the inflated ego seen in many CS students and some profs. CS courses are difficult.

Joe lectures are very unclear. He struggles to clarify basic concepts to students and the fact that he still struggles with the language makes him even harder to understand at times. Asking questions is virtually useless, because he will hardly ever give you an answer that makes sense/is related toyour actual question.

Joe cares a lot about his students and wants them to learn the material. He uses in class group activities a lot. Sometimes the lectures get boring when it is just him reading off of the slides, but he will always try to answer any questions you have. Also gives out candy. He said it was his first semester (Fall 2018) teaching as a professor.
Nice guy, but one of the worst teachers ever. Can barely read off of the slides.
He was an okay teacher. His lectures were okay, but he struggled to explain concepts well in his lectures. He is a very nice guy though :)
I honestly believe that he is a smart guy, but I learned absolutely nothing from his lectures. Practice exams were just spreadsheets of student submitted questions that weren't checked for accuracy.
Disrespectful and discouraging

If you truly want to learn something during grad school, take 689 with this prof. Both the lecture and the homework content were amazing. 689 is definitely going to take significant effort, and it assumes that you cover the necessary basics. However, the delivery of this challenging content was pretty good (provided you know your basics).

Transitioned to a fully online project-based course which helped to focus on core concepts and learning as opposed to exams and quizzes. One TA didn't explain concepts clearly, provided a confusing analogy, and when asked to clarify his explanation: "Sorry, I won't give you the answer. You'll have to figure out the solution for yourself."

Forced students to use his own library, whose syntax and usage weren't clearly explained. One of the TAs was really bad, made you feel dumb, and never told you if your solution was correct. I learned a lot, but I ended up spending significantly more time on this course than you typically would for a 3 credit one.

Only take this class if you're very comfortable with statistics and math. I can't emphasize that enough. Also be good at linear algebra and calculus. I'm very well versed in those fields but I can see why others are struggling and I would also personally struggle if I didn't know statistics well

HWs are easy if you know the math. Otherwise, avoid

Made this easy subject insanely difficult for no reason. Great TA, did most if not all of the work for the course. Absolutely unsympathetic to having deadlines extended. Assignments that take tens of hours that need to be completed within deadline or else its all for waste.

People will praise Tim Richards for not giving exams, but when Domke does the same thing but actually makes the assignments require the tiniest bit of effort, they'll scream bloody murder. Not to mention, Pangolin is 10x easier to use than base NumPyro. Be grateful. Your real issue is ChatGPT didn't see Pangolin in its training data.

I like how the course is structured and what materials are covered. However, the assignments often require knowledge not covered or partially covered in the lectures, which makes them time-consuming and sometimes too difficult as you have to learn it yourself. Yes, you learn a lot through the process, but the assignments are 100% of your grade...
People are just mad that they actually have to learn because he created his own package and can't use chatgpt. Lectures don't cover enough content though

Contrary to some beliefs - not a skill issue if the course is shambolic. Course staff is very condescending and never make you feel welcome. The instructor is heavily not involved. Questions on the homeworks (which account for all the grade) are dealt with as comments on his Notion page, or office hours with the TA which are full of snappy remarks.
Skill issue if you don't like him. Just adapt not that difficult.

No in-person/zoom lectures at all. He uploades his online videos instead. I never felt like I'm not learning anything to this extent. And I spend a lot of time to do hw.

We're supposed to learn from office hours which are held by TA's and not him. If I ask a question about my code, I get vague answers instead of a help to find my error.

Forces his students to use the python library he developed in the homeworks, which means we have to learn it. Skims over content expecting students to know it. Not very inspirational. I don't like being a hater but take a different professor

he's a great lecturer and i did learn a lot of ml from him. i do have a feeling that he gave people random grades though. really shady grading policy and never gave back final exams. a lot harder than other professors who teach the same course. would avoid him if possible.

My biggest concern with this instructor is that, unlike most educators I have encountered, he displays utter disregard for his students and their learning experience. Beyond delivering the scheduled lectures which stimulate little participation, he is removed from the learning process and provides no feedback on assignments. Quite disappointed.

One of the worst professors you can take a course under. Never gives any practice problems to learn things. Assignments are not enough for what he asks in the exams. The grading is unfair. The professor keeps telling he cares about students but doesn't even want to share correct solutions of the quizzes so that they can learn from their mistakes.
He's a great prof who gives quality assignments and really makes you think conceptually. His ML course is out of this world!

Good lecturer with interesting materials, but it was a bit hard to deal with him as a person. He (and TAs) grades only based on your answers and don't give any helpful feedback. HW sets were more like sklearn practice sheets. Lectures were helpful but not very much. I would take his course again but would avoid it if I can.

First of all, we had 6 homework assignments and 4 quizzes. The homework assignments took tens of hours to complete, but the 20 minute quizzes we did throughout the semester were worth, individually, MORE THAN the assignments. It's simply unfair to grade people in such a way. Course was completely disorganized, to the point that it was ridiculous.

Professor Domke doesn't have a Piazza for this course so asking for questions becomes difficult. There are too many quizzes and assignments and at least 2-3 questions in each assignment will not make sense at all. Grading is very harsh. The course material is good so I'd suggest you take this if someone else is taking the course.

Decent lecturer, homeworks were needlessly long and unclear, generally does not care about his students. The guy literally made piazza inactive as we were taking the final exam - most likely because he didn't want students to be able to give negative feedback about the handling of the class in an organized fashion. Did not handle COVID-19 well.

Short syllabus, great lecture notes, he knows his field cold. Logistical nightmares - no final exam scores released, intentional withholding of all solutions to HW/exams, completely inaccessible outside of class, dismissive and disrespectful to students who ask questions, handwavy about answers. Good topic, but would never work with him.

Wow, this guy has absolutely no respect for his students. No correct answers were given all semester (no solutions of any kind) and talking about homework assignments even past the due date is banned. He blatantly violates academic policy and only rectifies it on a student-by-student basis when directly confronted. Do not take this class to learn.

No syllabus/calendar provided. The instructor treats students with unwarranted inflexibility (even in Spring 2020) and disrespect (sometimes laughs at students' questions). Actively delayed final grading to prevent students from checking final grade in time to opt for Covid-19 Pass/Fail option. Just horrible to interact with.

Their grading policy was the "student will be evaluated on their final answer", meaning you will only get credit if your final answer is correct no matter whether your procedure is right or wrong. In other words, they are too lazy and unorganized to go through your answer sheet. The class was a big mess on a syllabus which is relatively small.
Easy class. Homeworks have bad document styles, hard to notice what should be reported on the final submission. Review some matrix operations + matrix calculus then this class should be easy.

He tries to create this sense of inferiority around him about being extremely inflexible (and yes, even in Spring 2020). We were literally wrapping up a topic on which our entire homework was based 3 days before the deadline (and yes, it was a fairly heavy assignment). Contrary to others, he covered no practical examples on the topics being taught.

Class wasn't bad in the start of the semester, but Domke made it clear that it would be difficult. Which is fine. But his TAs were utterly horrible, and getting feedback for homework took MONTHS. I have no clue how I'm doing on the last day of the semester. Shows no consideration for students during pandemic and no desire to make things any easier.

Assignments (made by TAs) basically have nothing to do with his lectures which are long and formula heavy. Never goes back to clarify what variables and symbols mean. Final exam has no partial credit despite the, by his own description, open ended nature of the questions. He gets irritated by students who are behind and ask "dumb" questions.
great lecturer. very clear about each topic. he will try his best to help you understand the materials. exam is pretty easy.

Awesome professor! Explains things very clearly and keeps the content interesting. Very good at explaining the big picture. The only complaint I have is that it can be a little hard to follow along what hes writing on the board sometimes because his handwriting is kinda small.

Worst professor I have ever had (graduate or undergraduate). He enjoys making the people around him feel inferior, and this motivation serves as the basis for his course. Instead of learning, I spent most of the time trying to figure out his expectations and do the tedious problems he assigned us. My 200 level undergrad ML class taught me more.

Professor Liberatore is my favorite and the best professor I've had so far. He's a great guy, and I love the way he explains things by drawing them out before coding it live so that you can easily grasp the concept of whatever he's teaching. The study guides are insanely helpful. I wish he taught all of my computer science classes.
Great teacher who cares about his students.
I absolutely love this guy!! I wish he took other CS classes and I'll be sad to leave him. Amazing professor w zero cons
Incredibly energetic and fun lecturer. He cares a lot about his students, and explains concepts simply and clearly the first time.
Goated professor. You'll actually want to go to class.
Great professor with very well organized course materials and energetic lectures. Having him as your first CS teacher is awesome.

Prof. Liberatore is a genuine, knowledgable and enthusiastic professor, who tries his best to make mundane topics interesting and engaging. As he puts it this class can be like eating your veggies, but he does his best to make it stick. I will say this isn't the most difficult CS class, but make sure to review your coding assignments before final
Probably the greatest CICS professor in UMass history. If you've never taken a class with him, do yourself a favor and sign up as soon as possible.
He's the goat

CS majors absolutely love this guy. He is friendly, young, and is just as much computer science as he is teacher. He obviously cares a lot about the course. He is the perfect balance of effective & reasonable.
Awesome, funny, engaging lecturer - love him to death.
Great guy, great teaching, yadda yadda you get the deal

Marc is one of the best professors I've ever had. He's so caring and really cares about his students and their learning. The class is quite rigorous, there are a lot of assignments and quizzes but if you manage your time well, the work reinforces concepts and helps with overall learning. He is super enthusiastic and brings a lot of energy to class

Marc is a great professor and is extremely enthusiastic about the course. The one thing that was bad with this course (which is reflected by my grade) was the grading system. Exams and quizzes make up around 70% of your grade, so if you're not great at writing out code like me it's very hard to get a good grade.
Excellent. Wish I worked harder. Felt as though the grades were earned.

Marc is one of the nicest professors I've met in the CS department so far. I thought the difficulty of the class was perfectly in the middle with some very difficult bits and other extremely easy parts. His lectures were generally pretty good and the notes he posted afterwards were extremely useful. Overall a very funny and personable guy.

Marc is such a kind and caring man and an amazing professor. The course is manageable, and Marc is very good at breaking down high-level concepts into bite-sized chunks, and letting the student come to their own conclusions/form their own thoughts. Projects are not weighted very heavily, but that might be a department thing, not a Marc thing.

I was lowkey fighting for my life in this class, but he's a great lecturer. Really funny and answers Campuswire posts quickly. The exams are fair, and he posts lecture notes. The concepts are difficult, but that's expected from a 187 equivalent. I learned a lot from him. 10/10 recommend

Marc is without a doubt one of the greatest professors on campus. He seems to genuinely care about his students. Marc tries to make lectures engaging and has plenty of patience. The course is fairly hard, but Marc is well aware of this and tries to make it as easy as possible for you.

Amazing professor. One of the best I have ever had. He is patient with students and is willing to help students as much as he can. Always bring passion to the class. He also tries to make exams and quizes relatively easy.
Best Instructor ever

Marc was an absolutely fantastic professor. This is probably the hardest course I have ever taken and if any other professor was teaching it I probably would've failed. Marc was a great and entertaining lecturer and also provided very detailed guides for the quizzes and exams. There were also weekly homeworks, but they had autograders, so not bad.
The classwork isn't too bad, and he is a very engaging professor. He has a great ability to take boring subject matter and make it interesting.
Marc is my Marius. He should always be teaching 230.
Great lecturer, very enthusiastic, assignments were well put together and pretty light.
Goated professor

Professor Liberatore was one of the best CICS professors I've had so far at UMass. His lectures were always entertaining to attend and expectations were made clear for a course as information dense as CS230. He always made sure to clear up any confusion with topics before moving on.

Energetic lecturer and a nice person, but terrible at course management. The class would have been all over the place if it hadn't been for the TAs. Exams do reflect course content but are graded harshly. We also could not see our final exam score and the overall course grade, so that was very frustrating.

computer science classes are hard. computer science exams are also hard. people are being silly by rating him bad for that universal truth; marc is a good guy. exams fairly reflect course content, if sometimes tricky. great lecturer. course staff was awesome. slay.
Absolute Legend of a man. No complaints at all. Wish all professors were like him.

Marc did a great job in making a previously easy class hard to score well. The exams are hard, they have a MCQ and an open response section. The MCQ's for one midterm were 3 points each, which means getting a few questions wrong would lead to a lot of points deducted. The grading for open response was harsh and not too much partial credit was given

Professor Marc has done an amazing job at making a class with pretty mundane (for most people) material and making it interesting. He maintains a pretty high energy level in lecture not seen from many other CS professors here, and is also pretty funny. The work and quiz/exam difficulty is not difficult if you study, compared to CS220.

Prof Marc is a legend, super funny guy, always gives extensions. The content in 230 can be boring at times but Marc has some great jokes in lectures. 230 is a decently difficult class, weekly quizzes are hard af study for those

Worst teacher. Takes forever to answer on Campuswire or at all. Doesn't accept emails. Doesn't give answers to practice exams. Doesn't give u back the exam sheets after exams. Information on slides are very much limited. The quizzes are graded out of 5 or 10 so they will tank your grade. Lots of quizzes and were very tricky.
Very fun lecture even when the content was boring AF! made the projects easier by giving lots of help

Probably the best CS prof at UMA. Marc is kind, understanding, and amazing at teaching. He will excuse assignments if you get sick and is very flexible. He also has a great sense of humor which makes lectures entertaining, and as a result makes it easier to learn the concepts.

Probably the best CS professor at UMass. Marc genuinely cares about the wellbeing of his students and gives extensions when needed. His lectures are one-of-a-kind; he explains difficult topics very throughly and clearly, and is very willing to answer questions. He is also very accessible to answer questions outside of class. He is a must pick.

Marc is one of the BEST professors I have had. Genuinely cares about his students' learning experience and grades. He is easily accessible and is ready to answer questions - response times via email or Campuswire were generally within 24-48 hours. Definitely recommend taking as many classes of his as you can!!!
Great teacher. Really helped me understand java at a deeper level, and I feel a lot more prepared for CS187. Really recommend his class if you passed 121, but are still confused about things

Fantastic instructor and genuinely nice person. He'll respond to any question you have and wants to see you succeed as a student. CS186 was challenging at times but everything was clear and manageable given that he grades based on 4 different metrics. Definitely take his class if you're interested in Comp Sci.

MARC IS THE MAN!!!! Really nice professor that cares for his students and you can tell how much effort he puts into every lecture by the energy he brings. Passionate and knowledgeable and sees students as people.
All around great guy, best professor I've had at UMass.

AMAZING GUY. Truly cares about each and everyone's success and wellbeing. If you have a valid reason for submitting homework late he'll give you enough time to do so. He is very understanding and caring, 10/10 would take again. Also, he looks sort of looks like discount Edward Snowden.

MARC IS AN AMAZING PROFESSOR! I have taken 3 classes with him and he is without a doubt the most competent professor I've come across within the CS department. He makes everything so easy to understand and really cares about your grade. He'll always give you extra time if you ask and he's such a great guy.

I am so glad I took this class my first semester - although the coding projects (1 each week, 11 in total) could be difficult, they also felt like fun puzzles. Lectures can go kind of fast since there's a lot of material, but Marc made the material interesting and engaging. He was also super helpful when I went for help during office hours.

Marc was a very fun professor during class and lectures, but when I would see him during office hours and after class, he did not seem as happy as usual, which I was not expecting. Overall, a very fun, relatable, professor!
Best teacher of this semester. The greetings made me fresh every class and I began to strongly love computer science. And he even teach a little python at end of the class. Amazing!

So far Marc has been my favorite professor. His lectures were interesting and in-depth and he was always open to questions after class. The class is assignment heavy so it was a pretty easy A if you put in the work as you can submit the projects as many times as you want. Weekly homework and quizzes in discussion and only a final worth 15%.

Lots of projects for this class but he's really lenient on deadlines as long as it's reasonable. Make sure you don't take the tests too lightly even though they're not worth too much. START PROJECTS ON TIME! This is the bulk of the work and if you try hard on them you'll do really well in this class
I have complained about my workload for Digital Forensics to my girlfriend so much, that we now use the word "forensics" as our safeword, because it is an instant turn-off.

I signed up for 365 thinking that it would just be another schmuck course that I would have to get through to get all my 300 requirements done. But with Marc's teaching, I actually found that material interesting. His lectures are easy to understand and riddled with humor. His lecturing truly made dry topics like low-level parsing interesting.

Marc is probably one of the best professors that I've ever had. There was homework due every class, but it was all very doable. His lectures were amazing. He puts his notes up after class and uses those instead of a textbook. He is super helpful in office hours and very approachable. 10/10 would take it again.

Marc is a great professor. He is really approachable, funny and takes effort to ensure that everyone understands what happens in class. He also had very low weight on tests and quizzes, and more on weekly homework projects.

Marc is an amazing computer science teacher and is very engaged in wanting to teach the material. He is very funny and made learning very enjoyable. He never asks for too much and will slow down if it is too confusing for the class. Take Marc if you can because he's amazing!
Fantastic teacher, mixes humor with learning makes the class engaging and fun. Lots of programming assignments but it's necessary for the class in order to learn.

There are way too many assignments and most of them test you on very similar things. There was a programing assignment every week. However Marc is super helpful if you go to his office hours and his lectures are interesting. The late policy was kind of messed up, but I don't think he'll continue it in future semesters.

The best teacher I have ever had for computer science in college. Clearly passionate about the subject, takes time for his students to understand every aspect of the material at hand, and is very accessible in person and online. I credit Marc for making me interested in CS again as well, phenomenal teacher and great person.
The best teacher I have had in my entire academic career.
BEST teacher. Made the COMPSCI experience for me
Hard class. Nice professor.
Marc is amazing! Best teacher I've had so far. 187 was great thanks to him. Very enthusiastic in lecture, and he's clearly passionate about what he's doing. He's young and stuff too, that helps

Marc is by far one of my favorite professors (or even) teachers I've ever had. He is always a breath of fresh air, and I look forward to the class regularly. His lectures are spot on, he even sometimes clearly writes out implementations of code you will be using in projects during class. He is always helping his students, and the tests are fair!
There is a LONG Coding Project Due every Week. So be Prepared to code, ALOT.

One of the best courses I have taken here as a CS student. Not easy by any means, but you will be a significantly better programmer, and leave the class with a strong understanding of the material. Tests are not that hard; class is mostly assignment-based.
Young and full of energy, makes the morning lecture enjoyable

Disorganized and ineffective. Lectures are rushed, unclear, and feel like information dumps with no examples or checks for understanding. Despite claims of responsiveness on Campuswire, he ignores important questions, even those critical for final exam prep. There's minimal support, and most learning is left to students to figure out on their own.
Marius is easily the goat.

Let us indulge in a social experiment. Invite your friends and family to take CS220 with Marius. If anyone complains, they should be removed from your life. Marius is a pleasure, and it is disheartening to see others blame their own shortcomings on him. Name a more iconic duo than Marius and Max. Their 2024-2025 run was one for the history books!

Very accessible outside of class, cares a lot about student success, but also blunt with high (but still reasonable) expectations. Reading slides BEFORE lecture helped immensely. Exams are hard but do lots of practice exams and recognize patterns in the questions and answers and you'll do fine. Curve is generous and extra credit is provided.

Marius is caring and easy to reach outside of class. He really tries to connect with his students. My issue lies with his lectures. His lectures contained significantly more material than the other class, and they were harder to understand. I had to read the other professor's slides and watch his recordings to understand the material.

Marius clearly cares about making his students better programmers, and expects a high level of effort from us. His lectures are exhausting and confusing, slides are way too dense and inconsistent. I enjoyed the lectures from the TA (Max) more than his. However, homework is designed well and helped me learn. Tests are crazy difficult of course.

The only redeeming quality about him is that he responds to CampusWire quickly. He explains concepts in confusing ways. Quizzes and exams are unreasonably hard. Argues with students when they give him a suggestion for the class. I would avoid this professor at all costs.

I have had him for 220 and 311: Syllabus and course has been set up very well/clear. He is always available for questions on Campuswire. 220 exams were unreasonably difficult, but 311 was not bad at all. Makes students critically think about content by asking questions. Slides are too much. He genuinely cares about becoming a better teacher.

I've had Marius for 220 and 311, and he genuinely wants you to be the best computer scientist you can be. His 220 exams are definitely unreasonably difficult and I don't even want to know what the 311 exams would have looked like without Sheldon, but it really seems like he wants to improve as a teacher, and he responds very quickly on campuswire.

Don't listen to anyone telling you to avoid Marius. You're at university to learn and be challenged right? Marius makes his class hard but he and his TA's do EVERYTHING to make sure students succeed. Amazing class.

I get it, the guy is caring. However, he is not a great teacher. There is more words on a single slide than the entire book of Genesis. Along with that, next to know live code. It is paying credits for a podcast. I just think he is someone who cares and tries, but his own intelligence holds him back from being a good professor.

Marius is quick to respond on Campuswire, which is great. Though his feedback can often be too verbose, as he wants to make the students think. There are many ways to LOSE points in this course (aside from the exams), whether that be from the quizzes, homeworks, or even class questions. You must work very hard for a decent grade, which is stressful
Marius is tough. Going to office hours is a little scary at first, but he actually gives really good feedback and will help you where you are at.

Marius is the best CS professor in the department. His class is difficult to make you better, that simple. He'll respond to you on Campuswire the night of a deadline within minutes, or stay late after class to answer your questions. Everything is designed to make you learn. The exams are difficult, but you'll get out what you put in.
He's the goat but if you're a bit slow you might not like him too much. Difficult exams but very fair grading and very knowledgeable guy in lecture
Exams are very difficult.

Marius is a tough but rewarding professor. His class requires serious work, but he provides abundant resources and support. I found Unstuckstudy to be a helpful study tool alongside the practice exams.

CS 311 with Marius was easily the most involved course I've taken at UMass in terms of coursework, but I learned a lot. Marius is always accessible outside of class and has a great deal of respect for those who try their best to engage with the course material.

Marius is a pleasant man who seems to care about student learning, but let's not pretend he's always been charming and lovable. His exams not being anything like the practice exams wasn't really a 220 aspect many people thought were reasonable. Still, his CW response speed is impressive.

This is a professor who will force you to work, and you will benefit from it. Clearly puts a lot of thought into designing assignments. Happy to work with anyone who will show up and put in effort. Very active and accessible on the class forum. One of those professors people either really like, or really don't (because they don't want to work imo).

Great lecturer, very accessible for office hours, responds very fast on Piazza, tough grader but curves fairly, difficult exams and projects but UCAs/TAs/Marius himself are very helpful. Learned the most in this class out of all my classes at UMass.

Honestly I think he's a great professor, but his classes are terribly hard and he makes them way more difficult than they need to be. But if you love learning hard concepts then you should totally take his classes.
It's a hard course but he's a good prof

Marius is a very challenging professor, but you will undoubtedly become a better programmer from CS220. He expects a lot out of his students which forces you to work harder, learn more, and find the most effective study strategies. My advice: Start projects early, practice old exams early (do mock exams all the way through), and go to SI sessions.
Start the projects early and you'll be fine.
Watched him yell at some kid for 5 minutes straight during office hours for being not knowing what to do in the coding assignment. 5/5
Still better than 240 with Mark Wilson

He is an extremely caring when it comes to student doing well. Never had a professor so accessible outside of class and makes it a point to answer questions in the middle of a lecture. Offers an abundance of resources as practice for exams. The content is not as bad as it is made out to be, but some effort is required.

HW is very doable, just start early and go to office hours. He cares deeply about the success of his students and will answer forum posts at very early hours/weekends, but please reread HW specs, other posts, and think before asking. However, his exams are out for blood, the best way to prepare is to do as many practice exams as possible, good luck

Marius' CS220 class has been one of the most defining parts of my time at CICS. I personally chalk the negative parts of CS220 up to the class material; ex. the topics tended to be uninteresting and lectures were very dense and fast-paced. Still, Marius displayed a great deal of care for his students and genuinely seemed invested in our learning.

Clear your schedule for this class- 10hrs/week of outside work on average
A lot will be expected of you, but you will learn a ton.
Lectures are clear but packed with information. I re-watched more than half of the lectures.
Work through the solutions to the practice exams to prep for exams
Use office hours, Campuswire, and free CS tutoring.
GL!
best professor in all of UMass

Professor Marius is a great lecturer and a tough exam writer. He is very accessible in office hours and on CampusWire. The class goes through material at an incredible pace, so you need to be 120% focused in lectures and review. Exams are where this class is difficult: the questions are very challenging. Homework is not hard if you put in effort.

Marius often responds to questions with vague answers, but he puts in a great amount of effort to always be available, while acknowledging that a question a student asked was good. The class is structured extremely well. Exam questions are challenging and often poorly worded, but not as difficult as project questions, and become enjoyable leetcode

From a teaching standpoint professor Minea is a great professor with an unique teaching style which forces students to learn and improve. CS220 with him was an extremely difficult course with tough exams/quizzes and strict grading. The weekly projects were difficult but doable and took up a large amount of time.

Every class you take with him will be very difficult, and you will get mad at both him and the content. Some people justifiably do not like how he talks, but he is a very caring person who wants his students to succeed. He requires thorough effort from students, but once you've taken multiple classes with him, you'll realize how great he is.

Marius is a great person&truly cares about his students' learning; has high expectations of students! Always active on Campuswire. Grading & the class is generally well structured, but the exams/work load will make you want to drop. Exams are so difficult he himself has said he's worried about people not passing the 33% threshold.

Nothing I can say that hasn't been said. If you take a Marius class, you will get out what you put in. Difficult exams, lots of homework, but you'll absolutely learn a lot of material. Isn't that why you're at college?
He's a very nice guy, if not a little short in his Campuswire responses. But he genuinely wants students to succeed.

Have taken 2 classes with Marius (220, 311). Marius is a very caring and intelligent man. He teaches infamously difficult classes, hence so many ratings. His exams and homework are a survival mission. But he gives a solid and reliable teaching performance. He will never let you fail if you put in the effort. Pros and cons ofc, but 150% worth it.

Literally makes the class impossible for no reason. Makes the quizzes so dumb because half the stuff isn't even useful and isn't from the slides most of the time. You just have to know it which makes no sense because where am I supposed to learn it from when they are supposed to teach the material? Exams are even crazier and impossible.

Prof. Minea is one of the best teachers I've ever had. His lectures are engaging, with great examples and applications from real world to help us better understand the abstract concepts. Exams are not too hard, as long as we have prepared.

For those reading, yes, Marius's rating is probably terrible with 1 star reviews.

But if you ever wanted to take a course and get something out of it, there's no prof who does it like Marius.

Any of Marius' courses have difficult exams and homeworks and you will need to work extra. But also there's excellent communication and accommodation.
I've taken two classes with Marius. and I have to say that the classes I've taken with him are definitely the hardest I've taken, but I've also learned way more in them than any other class.

Marius comes across as rude sometimes, but he does seem to care. He's extremely responsive to Campuswire questions. The projects are well-designed and doable within a week. The quizzes and in-class questions, on the other hand, are ridiculously difficult, poorly written, and not a good indicator of whether you'll do well on the projects or exams.
easiest class of my life. we watched movies.

220 with Marius can definitely be considered a weed-out class. Although the contents covered are actually very interesting and useful in the field of computer science. However, some concepts are very abstract and you need to work your brain to understand it. Quizzes are difficult, exams are hard but mostly reasonable. Great officer hours however.

The main downside to Prof. Marius is the extremely difficult quizzes and exams. The only good thing is he posts many past exams to review from but the exam averages will still be deadly. He does apply a curve on overall class grade. Lectures are boring and long

Marius's UCAs deserve to get berated for caring more about massaging their ego by defending his behavior and abstract teaching style instead of helping students thrive and listening to reasonable student concerns.

Pros:
- Rsponds so quickly to CampusWire posts, very easy to reach for help
- Will host spontaneous office hours if needed
- Genuinely cares about the learning of his students.
- Notes have high detail

Cons:
- Exams are so hard
- Quizzes also hard, with the bonus of little to no feedback
- Can come across as mean. I don't think it's intentional
This man is accessible nearly 24/7 on campuswire. He may be a tough grader (the exams are deadly), but I really feel that I learned a lot more from this class than most others.
literally the best teacher ive ever had in my entire life

Always supportive when I came to talk about HW after class, praising good Qs and great response time on forum, advocating for my interests. Timed quizzes and lengthy exams took a yr off my life, but considering I'm not the only one who doesn't test well under pressure, all worked out with weighted grades. Liked his interactive lecs w/ code.

No exercises to bridge the gap between lectures and his expectations for exam/quiz problems. No resources could help you deduce what he would throw at you. Got frustrated with student questions during office hours. Answered genuine questions with new questions. Probably a good guy sometimes, but not all the time. Literally just relieved I passed.
Failed my first ever class thanks to him:) The concepts are easy when someone else teaches them to you but he makes it extremely hard. Exams are hard. HW's are hard. Lecture's are boring.
Challenging homeworks and exams, but if you try to learn, he is extremely helpful with answering questions. He is very knowledgeable so asking him any sorts of doubts will teach you something new.
Quizzes and exams are brutal, homework instructions are never clear.

TLDR: AMAZING TEACHER, TOUGH GRADER. I took him for CS 220 in fall 2022. The course was one of the best designed courses I've taken so far. However, the exams were almost designed to make you fail, so it did require a lot of reading. Even though it was a hard class, I can say I learned something real in this class.

Really difficult exams and quizzes. HWs are okay with lots of TAs and UCAs for extra help (+ campuswire). Lecture style is boring but the slides are good. Marius is a notoriously tough grader who will make sure you struggle but he's a really nice guy so don't be afraid to reach out for help. Also gives extensions for valid reasons.
Negative reviews=bad grade before the curve. He is the best professor in all of UMass by far
Marius is POG
He is amazing

Great professor who challenges his students. Most classes at umass are taught formulaically with the exams being close to the practice exams. Marius actually makes them different so the test is reflective of your knowledge instead of memorizing the practice exams.
Everyone who writes negative reviews just got a bad grade and they are mad. His curves are ridiculously generous. Trust me. Super nice and very approachable.
Horrible. Try no to take his class unless you are a CS nerd

Marius needs to learn to respect others like he was already respected. Jumping on to students is wrong, they could have had serious bad luck with their lives that semester, and the least they need is further childish behavior by a grown man. Students defending his behavior are probably UCAs attempting to uplift their privileged fragil ego again.
Marius is a good guy, but don't take too many credits while taking his classes because he is very impatient with people who have questions in his office hours (very different person).

Marius really, really cares about his students. He can be strict and is maybe not the best professor for everyone, but he is an EXCEPTIONAL person who will follow up with you and talk and listen. CS220 is a horrible horrible class that I hate with all my heart, but I don't think Marius really has much to do with it.

Marius knows the material, but he doesn't know how to explain it. While he puts in a lot of effort for students to succeed, he isn't helpful in his responses when you ask him or reach out to him. The TAs were friendly and helpful, but the UCAs are rude and incompetent. The whole structure and design of his classes needs some serious overhauling.

I agree that you can't be spoonfed, but the reality is that Marius Minea is not a good professor. He may be a smart man but he does not know how to teach. Not every smart guy is a good teacher. His UCAs are very rude and care more about inflating their ego and ignoring valid constructive feedback than setting students up to succeed.
Good teacher. Only downside is he's way too hard on his students. His scales make up for it though.
really caring guy. works tirelessly to make sure that you learn. reach out to him and he will help

Great professor, anyone saying that "Marius sucks" are just soft people who expect hand-holding. Put in the effort, and you'll do fine. Marius wants to pass his students. Don't be soft, and don't expect hand-holding.

Ok this review is being left to address all of the 1-star reviews that convey the "waaah they didn't spoon-feed us" message. It's college, instructors aren't supposed to spoonfeed you their subject matters anymore. The nature of CS is that it's a difficult major. You have to work for your grade. His classes are tough, but a curve makes up for it.

He's a good professor who taught me the art of asking good questions. The people who say otherwise are the ones who expect to be spoonfed. Put in the effort, and you'll be ok. You cannot be spoonfed by your professor.

He's not the best, but I think he still deserves a 5 because he's a good person, he's learning how to be a better professor after acknowledging genuine student feedback seriously, and has at least a decent level of empathy towards students. The 1-star ratings have an ulterior motive because Marius isn't that bad anymore.
Genuinely the best teacher I've ever had. Go to his office hours and you will understand everything. I have never had a better teacher.

Overall, an ok dude. Marius may seem to care about student learning and answer questions fast, but unfortunately this gets overshadowed by his inability to construct meaningful assignments and exams for students. The course design of his classes needs a serious update.

Marius doesn't deserve this bad of a reputation. Yes, his classes are hard, but that's how it's supposed to be in college. He is not supposed to spoonfeed you his wealth of knowledge and subject matter. You have to put in the effort.

Marius is okay overall. He is very thorough in his lectures, as well as a knowledgeable person when it comes to questions, but at the same time it can be extremely intimidating to approach him. He expects a lot from his students (even for underclassmen), and asking somewhat basic questions are met with sharp replies.

I've taken 3 classes with him, worked with him as an assistant for one, and will take another next semester. Some might find him mean, but he really does care about his students. His homeworks and exams are harder than when other professors teach it, but I find it the most challenging and made me want to learn more.

Marius is a tough guy whose classes are demanding and getting an A is no easy task. BUT he does care and wants you to succeed. Marius is very accessible as he has at least two shadow clones constantly monitoring and answering questions on Piazza, Campuswire, etc 24/7. There is a person who keeps spamming him with bad reviews, don't listen to them.
Terrible! Designed the HW and Exam in unnecessary hard level, the hint of HW is to read the problem and textbook carefully.

Marius has made computer science, something I used to have a strong passion for, completely intolerable for me.
The class is too difficult and goes by without sufficient coverage of complex material. Marius also gets noticeably frustrated when asked basic questions.
I will never voluntarily take another class with Marius again.

311 is one of those classes that will drain your soul, and Marius will make it 10 times harder. Will he help you learn the content? For sure. Will he stress you out? Definitely. Will it be worth it in the end? I hope so. Exams so far have been fair, but the HWs will make you want to cry to sleep every night. Textbook is a must-read along with lecs
No offense to Marius as he seems like a good guy, but I could never learn from a professor who is way too unclear in lectures.
Marius isn't terrible at teaching, but to be honest he's too unclear, and I could never learn from a professor who just reads off slides directly without explaining the concepts in new ways.

The class is challenging but for the right reasons. Lectures are pretty interesting and lab exercises are good practice. The most useful CS class I have taken at UMass by far. The content is difficult and getting good at programming practices is difficult but the class is more than fair with dropped HWs, and quizzes. Great response time.

Marius makes his classes harder than they need to be, but he really cares and is always fast with communication. His classes are extremely difficult, but in exchange you’ll boost your work ethic by being careful when asking questions. Plus, he scales everything to make up for the difficulty of the class.

Marius makes his classes harder than they need to be, but he really cares and is always fast with communication. His classes will kill you, but in exchange you’ll boost your work ethic by being careful when asking questions.

Marius doesn’t deserve the review bombs anymore. Yes, his exams are hard, but he’s very receptive to criticism, which is rare in many CICS professors. He began his redemption arc after the switch to in-person learning. The way he explains stuff could improve, but he’s doing his best.
220 has a lot of interesting material, but Marius singlehandedly took the fun out of that class through his dry and formal teaching style and condescending view towards helping students.

Severely underrated professor. His classes are hard, but final grades are scaled, lectures are interesting, and he cares a lot and puts in a lot of effort to help his students learn. Marius may seem stern, but he is also one of the most organized CS professors I've had, which is very rare in the CS department at UMass.

I was scared to take a class with Marius after hearing/reading the negative comments, but I was so pleasantly surprised. His classes are challenging, but lectures are engaging and he puts in a lot of effort to help his students thrive. He may seem severe, but Marius is the most compassionate prof I've had in the CS department at UMass.
I'm glad I didn't take 311 with Marius. Marius sucks at teaching, and his lectures are dry and a genuine waste of time. Don't expect any help from him. He's awful. Awful awful awful.

Marius isn't great at teaching but I don't think he's terrible either. Personally I found reliance on 220 peers to be more helpful than him, but I still went to his lectures to learn new knowledge. Marius's rendition of 220 taught me that the best tip to do well on the programming projects is to start them early and work with a peer for each one.

220 was lowkey a rewarding class for software engineering in terms of material learned, but Marius's lectures weren't very helpful for the difficult homeworks and exams as the lectures only brushed over the basics. If more online resources such as practice problems for each topic were posted, the workload for 220 would've been more bearable.

Marius expects a lot from his students because he thinks so highly of them. In our first class he was so happy for us to be in the classroom again eager to learn. I respect professors who make people work, it's kind of rewarding. He's always available through campuswire and though sometimes blunt, he will never not help you.
Marius cares and his classes have the most generous curves, but that doesnt compensate for his poor teaching skills whatsoever

Marius Minea is a real joke of a professor. I went to his office hours, and Marius didn't help at all; he just wanted to lose patience with me and make me uncomfortable to ask questions in order to boost his ego. This attitude is not at all conducive to a welcoming work environment. I would never take classes with Marius ever again.

Good Prof. Knowledgeable, will take time to explain concepts again if people actually ask questions. Exams are really difficult, but the curve is always generous, and unlike popular wisdom, I don't think "more people fail" in his courses. Recalibrate your expectations for exams, you'll learn a lot and your grade will be fine.

He is by far the best Professor Ive ever had. His exams are hard but his curves are overly generous frankly. Super accessible outside of class ( unmatched). Super easy to get a B but you have to work for an A.
Marius clearly knows his stuff, but has no teaching skills whatsoever

Stay away from him at all costs. He's condescending, his difficulty jumps from lectures to the homework is too much, and it's just stressful all around. Marius has a reputation for making hard classes harder. Maybe it's cause he cannot explain difficult topics. Maybe it's because he just cannot teach. Just a thought

The only reason I'm giving a Marius a 2 and not a 1 is because I feel he was very accessible on campuswire and he was very helpful in answering questions. However in lecture, he was just too confusing . Quizzes were impossible and stressful too, even though they're supposed to be a "learning opportunity". Final exam was way too long as well.

He makes the class difficult beyond practicality. Lectures were super simple, yet quizzes and tests were 100x more brutal than even practice tests so you never knew what to expect. The only way people get a decent grade is because he puts a massive curve. His facade of wanting to improve is fake since he resets his changes each semester.

Professor Minea changed in a solid way this semester. He is a strong lecturer that knows what he's talking about. He was very available always on CampusWire and office hours if you didn't understand the material. The tests for this class were VERY hard, but he scaled every one of them.

His classes go fast, in depth, and his tests/homeworks are hard. That's all true. But if you really want to learn, you will learn the most from his classes. The habits his courses hammered into my programming really had a positive impact at internships and interviews. The classes are insanely difficult for sure-- but you will learn so much.

Marius really makes you learn the material to pass the class. Overall I really enjoyed his lectures even though he sometimes can struggle to explain hard-to-understand concepts in a simple way. He can also be very passive-aggressive which can rub people the wrong way, but I found it very entertaining. Always available on campuswide, would recommend

I think hes a . Yeah hes not perfect... it can be easy to space out at his lectures since he often explains things in a very roundabout, hard to understand way. Tests and quizzes can also be VERY hard. But its clear he cares about trying to teach us, esp with how much he answers questions online. Pretty ok dude

Marius seems to care about his students, answering questions quickly on campuswire and trying his best to improve himself with the feedback from previous semesters (though it seems hes got a lot to improve). However, while I did like his lectures, they were not helpful for assignments and tests because they were that hard.
Easily the best professor in CICS. His class is extremely difficult but you'll never find a professor like him that genuinely wants to see you succeed and actually cares that you learn the material
Easily the hardest class I've taken at UMass. Also, the most I've learnt. 311 is a must with Marius if you want to learn CS.

Marius' classes are brutal, there's no denying that. His homework, quizzes and exams are probably the hardest there are. In the moment his classes are extremely stressful, but looking back you will be glad you took the class with him. He will make you a better programmer. He's very knowledgeable, but can also be very brash. Expect to be challenged.

Quiz: you would think the lecture is enough to go through the materials and prep you for the quiz; not true. His weekly quizzes are PAINFUL and worse because you only have 30 minutes to complete. Homework: His homeworks are lengthy and extremely hard because you have to think out of the box for every single one. Only 10% is lecture related.

He's like Anton Chigurrh: cold but calculated. Well he seems cold. Get to know him better and he's really sweet and supportive. He is a bit controversial, though. On one hand he's very responsive to your questions, but oftentimes the onus is on you to read the instructions, to which he will point you back to. You need to take him if you are in CS.

This has been the hardest class I've taken ever. However, if you really want to challenge yourself you will learn many things from Marius. The class will force you to learn time managment skills, become a better programmer, and be a better leaner. I only recommend if you truly want to push yourself. His tests are insane, but final grades are fair.

Marius is tough. His homework and exams are long and difficult. But if you understand the material cold, you will get an A. He is very very quick to respond to issues and provides all the materials you need to succeed. If you want to learn a lot, take this class. If you want an A, then don't.
Do NOT take his class! It's toooooo difficult, and generally it is hard to get help from him. The good aspect is that he replies questions very quickly, but those answers are too vague to understand.

One of the best classes ever. I'm way better at programming bc of this class. Many ppl just trynna collect a bunch of A's, but if you actually want to learn and grow, Prof Minea is the move. Exams are VERY difficult, but final grading is VERY fair. Also prof is ALWAYS on online to answer Qs. I've never seen such a dedicated Professor. Highst Respct

I think Marius is being rated unfairly here. Yes, the tests make you think, but are very possible if you throughly understand material. 2nd midterm was a bit too long. The pre-recorded lectures were some of the best I've seen at Umass. Concise and clear, but most importantly this class is good training. It will make you a better coder.

Marius is a very intelligent and well-meaning professor that has a six sense for Campuswire, makes you work for a good grade, and wants you to learn. However, these intentions are often lost in translation because of the difficulty of his renditions of the courses, particularly in exams and homework, dubbed the "Marius Effect". Good guy, but stern.

Marius is a solid professor. You need to put in lot of effort to get a good grade. This semester he didn't curve any exam and I don't think he will even curve the final, which makes it really difficult to get a good grade. If you really wish to learn something and don't care about grades take this course with him otherwise donot.
