After midterm 2 where the exam was too long to be solved in a reasonable time, Marius apoligized for it and promised to make the final exam more manageable and to lower the weight of midterm 2 if we do better on the final one. Guess what - the final exam was another unmanageable one due to the length. Marius never changes.

Let's Start with the pros: - He is incredibly intelligent - He is very responsive Now the cons: - He is unable to simply explain anything - He can be combative if you fail to understand something - He makes things needlessly complicated Taking this class was one of the most stressful experiences of my life and I have been robbed at gunpoint.

The problem with Marius is that he does everything he can to not help you. Asking simple questions leads to unhelpful responses. He's very active on campuswire but doesn't give helpful responses. Not to mention the exams are impossibly hard.

Minea isn't awful; but I think he expects you know more than the average student does coming into this course. He answers questions on campuswire really fast, but the answers aren't always useful, as is the case with many CS professors here. The exams are tough, but the homework are fairly doable. All in all not a terrible experience.

Marius cares about his students and always answers questions quickly. That's about as far as the good stuff goes. He is horrible at creating exams, bad at explaining concepts, and is incredibly condescending and rude. I have had him for 2 semesters and now I go out of my way to avoid courses that he is teaching.

Marius is extremely vague with his lectures by presenting them as if everyone has already come to class understanding the material beforehand and then getting annoyed when you don't understand it fast enough to his standards. He also writes homework and tests as if he were purposefully trying to make the final curve as large as possible.

Marius cares. He might be a really caring person, but he WILL make you work for a good grade in his classes, unlike most professors at this college, but you will learn so much more. Homework and exams are definitely difficult, though whenever I was confused in class, he's been able to explain things to me in a way that makes sense.

You won't succeed if this is your first exposure to js, especially since most of his functions are written in one long line, as for this wonderful midterm you basically had to code a hw project from scratch and answer some other questions during the span of 2hrs when a hw project usually gives you a week to submit it. His lectures are also vague.

Marius never learns from his mistakes. He already knew that his midterm exams are nearly impossible to finish in 2 hours. Some might argue that he is honest and always admits to his mistakes. However, he never actually does anything to improve. Try to avoid him as much as possible.

His tests are nearly impossible to finish even if you have many years of programming under your belt, and his wording is very hard to understand on the exams and just general responses. HOWEVER, with all this being said this has forced me to watch every lecture 100 times and practice problems over and over again making me much better at coding.

He's a good professor, he clearly cares about students understanding the material. He may often come off as condescending but be patient with him and he'll leave you with a solid understanding of the material. Midterms are absolutely terrible though, extremely hard and/or lengthy, but that doesn't discredit his ability to teach.

It's quite an enjoyable class so far. The projects are engaging and Marius does have nice sense of humor despite giving quite a scary first appearance for me. The midterms can be a bit too much work if you came in unprepared though. It might be too much work for just 2 hrs that you can barely finish on time

Marius tries very hard, a bit too hard perhaps. The questions he comes up for homework and tests are intentionally very hard, but he knows that and will not expect you to answer everything perfectly. So no need to freak out if some questions seem too hard. The scale is usually more than generous if you can score above average consistently.
Marius is even more vague in 311 than he is in 250. The lectures sound very easy but the homwork is a million times harder than the lecture itself.

Is vague in lectures, doesn't know how to explain the material and God forbid you go to him confused on a subject. He'll scoff at you and get visually annoyed if you don't understand the material after his first confusing explanation

Marius is a caring professor and does try to help to make students succeed. However, he fails to do so. You are better off reading the book (which is already very confusing) than watching his lectures. He does answer everyone's piazza posts very quickly but they are usually not helpful at all or come off as rude. Please avoid him if you can.

Marius answers the questions on Campus Wire really quickly, but his answers are mostly cryptic and will not help much unless you have already understood all of the materials. The exams are on another level and is roughly as hard as a homework which would take several days to solve.

The main redeeming quality that Marius has is that he always tries to answer students' questions quickly. Unless you really understand the material to begin with, you won't benefit from his explanations in or out of lecture. His exams are also extremely disproportionately difficult, and there is nothing that can properly prepare you for them.

He responds to emails and piazza pretty quickly, I totally respect that. But the problem is his responds do not help solving the problem, it makes you really confused and you might wonder why are you even doing this. He even did a apology to us regarding to students feedbacks for being condescending and superior during office hours.

Marius is a great prof. He gives very quick responses on piazza and is very knowledgeable which I respect. The problem of his class is the difficulty gap his lectures and his exams. His lectures are like : introduction of wrenches; homeworks are like: use wrenches to crack walnuts; his exams will be: make an air jet with wrench. (0_0)

Marius is a responsible professor for answering questions actively on piazza. But he made the course too difficult for students to learn, and you won't perform well on the exam even if you take it very seriously. If you have a lot of coding experiences, you will be fine. But if not, you will struggle and worry about homework and exams all day.

Marius is a solid Professor. CS 250 itself is a difficult course and no one can blame Marius for that. I have seen people talk bad about him, but he is a real gem. He is dedicated and will try to help you as much as possible. Sometimes he gives arcane answers, but that makes you think about the question in depth. Definitely recommend him!

Maybe its because we are online now but Marius is the worst professor I have had so far. I have him for both 220 and 250 and his teaching style simply does not work for me or anyone else I know. It is clear he cares and tries his best to be on piazza as much as possible. However, it does not make up for his inability to teach the material.

Marius is a brilliant Professor, but it might not be immediately apparent. He seems to be the type of guy who is too smart for us plebian students. It puts off some students initially, but that's good news since sometimes his explanation is too intelligent for us, but he always slows down when we ask :D

First sem sophmore CS major. CS250 is the hardest class I've ever taken. Difficult content, very long homework assignments, stressful exams. Marius often gives arcane answers when you ask him questions. If you're interested in the subject, excellent opportunity to learn, as marius goes in depth. Otherwise, god have mercy on your soul.

Man may look intimidating but don't let that fool you, he's got a heart of gold. He sneezes everytime someone makes a Piazza/Campuswire post and if he's not on there answering your once again pointless question for the n-th time, then I don't know who else will. Great guy, very helpful, passionate about teaching.

This was his first semester teaching 220, and the class was more difficult than any of us expected. The homework was easy; the exams were awful. I know he puts in a lot of effort into the course (he responds really quickly on Piazza), which I respect, but he can be condescending. I was so stressed about passing just to end up with an A-

Well meaning Professor even though he wildly miscalculated the difficulty to make 220 this semester. Although 220 is no longer a breeze, I think the takeaways from this class as compared to previous iterations are more valuable. As long as you work hard enough and put in enough of an effort, the class is doable. He responds to piazza questions fast

Marius really cares and wants the best out of his students. He really pushes you to get the most out of the class. He responds very quickly on piazza and is really helpful in office hours. However, he expects a lot and can come off really condescending or abrasive. Just be prepared to work hard. He made this class super hard since it was his 1st se

Marius is a good guy but I find that his lectures are pretty awful. He lectures as if everyone already knows what he's talking about and so most people tend to zone out during lecture. He's also very unforgiving. If you turn in your homework 2 seconds late, sucks for you. You get a 0. In my opinion if you have an alternative, don't pick him.

Marius is a super tough professor, but he really does care and want you to learn and apply yourself. He will respond in a matter of minutes even at late hours. While I do feel that this course itself was kind of stupid this semester in terms of exams not matching up with HW, I am confident that Marius will continue to improve the course.

A great teacher who is very generous to give hard-working student A. He is very happy to answer your questions on pizza. His CS220 and CS250 and CS311 are highly recommended. Study hard and get an A for yourself.
If you really want to learn something, please take him CS250 and CS220.
You are able to get A, if you study hard and have some luck. You can learn a lot. He is always waiting online to response your questions.

Emphasis on the "Would take this professor again", despite 3 stars. His classes will be hard and he will make you work for it, but he is one of the most responsive, adaptable, and caring professors I've had.

I don't think Marius is as bad as people say. This class is definitely harder than most people expected this semester, but in all honesty the difficulty of course material is on par with CS 187. He responds extremely fast on Piazza, and puts in a lot more effort into his teaching than most professors I've had. Don't be deterred by the bad reviews.

Makes the exams unnecessarily hard. Talks about real-world problems as an excuse for anything in a sense that only benefits himself and not the students. Advisors ask us to take this course with 250 which is also really hard and historically this course (220) was easy but Marius just wanted to ruin that.

Hw is very easy. First exam was 5x harder than any hw, practice material or lecture material we received. The median on that was an F which should speak for itself. Makes it very hard for the average student to keep up and learn. Moves at an extremely fast pace for the level of understanding he expects us to have.

This professor is awful in clearing the homework materials. Homework never been easier and posting piazza questions and the feedback from this professor never clear about homework. Homework materials and questions are not clear and have to figure unrelated course materials/ wasting more time on it.

Definitely not the easiest class. Overall, it isn't too difficult in the class if you pay attention in the class and put in the work. Marius is pretty reasonable and really cares about how students are doing in the class.

Great professor, he puts in the work to help students reach his standards and scales the class well. 250 is tough with the workload and confusing concepts, but as long as you put the work, ask questions, and pay attention, you will do fine.

Tough class with the workload and the material itself, but he goes above and beyond in helping students understand topics. He answers each and every question and you can tell he wants his students to succeed as long as they put the work in.

Before taking this course, I came into it being told that it was the hardest of the 200 series CS classes. I know understand why. The course has bi-weekly problem set homeworks. They are hard, start early, ask questions on piazza, and I would recommend doing them in a group. READ AND REREAD THE TEXTBOOK AND SLIDES TO UNDERSTAND THE MATERIAL.

Marius' class was consistently a worry for me. I thought throughout almost the entire course that I was going to do poorly as the grading curve was never clearly defined. Leaving every exam I thought I was going to fail them, and ended up doing about average. So don't wory until you see your exam grade.

Marius is a very to the point professor. In my opinion, UMASS could use more professors like him. I've had professors who don't put in half as much effort as him. The course may be slightly challenging, but he will give a very fair curve in the end. If you're expecting to just do homework and do well, this course is not for you.

Marius is a good professor who takes his job very serious, but is way too hard on his students. He is not very helpful in office hours (tries to hint but does not do so effectively) and gets extremely frustrated with dumb questions, so be prepared. Expect 30 hours a week from you. Overall, very very tough, but good professor (super tough grader)

Pros: -Responds quickly -Understands the material well -Is responsive to suggestions -Flexible accessibility Cons -Dry and condescending -Very tough grader -Exams are hard to prepare for -Does not effectively teach most of the class, rather focused on the top students.

Challenging but rewarding class. I had a hard time following the material, but to give him due credit I got a lot out of it at the end. Looks like he was very fair at the end with grades too, in spite of a tough semester. For all the tough material, he was very sincere and went out of his way to hold review sessions.

Probably the worst professor I've ever had. Can't fathom how anyone could even do homeworks without some heavy help from online. Exams are equally hard. Lectures are extremely hard to follow and something presented for 1 minute could be on the exam. Takes no feedback even when he asks for it, and gets frustrated when asked too many questions.

Bad teacher all around. His lectures often left people very confused on the topic. If people did not understand the concepts and asked questions, he often got visibly frustrated with us. I'll give him some credit and say that he did raise how much the homework is worth when we complained about and listened tried to complaints in general.

Demeaning to students and honestly hindered the learning of the course content. I'd give him credit for setting the bar high for the class which means you have to learn a lot to pass, that being said he does not help you reach that bar. Cling to your TAs/textbook tightly as they will be the only thing from stopping you from drowning in this course.

Honestly, I think the reviews given for Marius are not accurate. Sure the class is hard but Marius tries really hard to explain concepts and really cares that every student learns. I think he got feed up towards the end of the semester just because so many students stopped trying and caring. The tests are hard but the class is scaled.

Marius is a terrible professor. He might be good for 250 because he was easier than Barrington, but that's his only positive. It's clear that he doesn't care about how well his students do, and tries his hardest to make students feel dumb. In midterm 2, he had an outburst over somebody asking a simple (although obvious) question. Avoid him for 311.
Marius has not been very helpful thus far. He doesn't seem to understand the struggles of his students. He also does not have a very clear method of grading and grades exams/homework very harshly.

The class is probably the hardest you would have done so far, but Marius goes a long way in making it enjoyable (by not being Barrington). He is definitely better paced, and his smaller class size helps a lot. Pro tip: Have a friend in the other class to help you with clicker questions, cause Marius gives you like no time to think about them.

Great teacher that cares about students and cares about them understanding material. He is accessible and the tests always match what is taught in class (though the final was a bit rough). Would recommend and I hear Barrington is also good. Lots of long homework but doing it pays off.
one of the best to do it

Professor Golin's lectures were really well organized and clear. It was easy to follow along and I rarely felt unclear about anything, but when I did he answered my questions thoroughly. The projects were fun but a little challenging towards the end; as long as you manage your time on them you should do great in the class.

Lectures were great and involved slides instead of live coding and were very comprehensive. Exams were not too hard and are basically the quizzes that were taken during the lab sections. Overall great course.

I went to Mordecai's lectures because I couldn't follow Barrington, and I felt he explained concepts in a way that was easier to follow. He is also really helpful one-on-one if you go to his OH, as he will explain things in many different ways until you understand. Discussions, projects, and tests are all pretty hard, so he is a good resource.

I found the lectures to be incredibly boring/overcomplicated and they weren't much help for me. However one thing Mordecai did really well was provide study guides that told you EXACTLY what you needed and didn't need to know for every quiz and exam which made the class very easy.
He's great, very passionate guy. Don't sit near the speakers in the lecture hall, he coughs A LOT and it's so loud and it might even spread it you.

Mordecai is a very clear lecturer and quite accessible after and outside of class. He clearly cares about his students' success and the grading on exams and homework assignments has a very fair curve. Attendance is taken with ClassQuestion. Expect to spend a lot of time on the homework, it's a property of the course, not the instructor.

His lectures are super clear. definitely take him. A lot of people say this class is really hard, but honestly I think it's just the workload. The grading for both homework and exams has a lot of extra credit, so it's not that hard to get a good grade.

I love Mordecai. 250 is a difficult course but it's my favorite I've taken at UMass, all thanks to him. His lectures are clear, well-paced, and relevant to the homework/exams. The tests in this class are quite straightforward, just start homework early and ask for help when stuck. Definitely take him instead of Barrington, or just go to his lecture
I have no gripes with this guy. Class sucks a ton though.

Professor Mordecai is a good DSA professor. His lectures and slides are well organized. The slides are really informative, but the lecture is a little slow-paced. He does not post any past exams, but his study guide contains all the content you need to know for the exams and quizzes. He is also really accessible via email.

Professor Golin is by far the best Professor I've had at UMass. His lectures are amazing, and he's also very caring about his students- always tries to solve everyone's questions during exams himself. He made 210 and 250 much easier to navigate and study despite the very difficult course load. He's also super helpful via office hours and email.
Very good lecturer. Projects were interesting, lab assignments were helpful, and exams were fair.

The homework is a LOT and the material is not always the easiest, but he does a good job teaching the content. The lectures can feel a bit unfocused on occasion but they're usually well delivered and informative. Ultimately he does a very good job teaching, and the hard part of the class is the workload rather than understanding his lectures.

250 has a reputation as a really hard class, but if you go to lecture and understand the HW assignments it's not bad at all. Golin makes a lot of the more advanced concepts really easy to understand, even if the lectures are at a fast pace.

Professor Golin is a genuine, helpful person. The lectures were very faced paced and can seem intimidating. To prepare for exams, review previous year's exams (this year's were almost identical to last year's). If you do not have a group for the homework assignments, it's going to be rough. Overall, very time consuming course but interesting.

Very nice guy! Class was structured VERY well. Study guides reflected the exams well. I honestly learned alot from this class and enjoyed it. Everything is organized on Canvas too. Just a really good professor

One of the most caring professors you will meet. He is very kind, and would answer your question right after class. I really appreciate the extra office hours he held before exams. He also explains concepts well. I would definitely want to take more courses with him, but it seems he only teaches 210 and 250.

the class was structured very well- very straightforward, never caught by surprise on tests or homework's and they actually show u exactly what u did wrong and the right answer, but golin is kind of a mean guy:( you will do good in the class, the tas and si's are super good and he def cares about teaching and puts all the material u need!

Difficult material but very rewarding if put in the work. Start the homework as soon as possible. I made a huge mistake waiting til last day of first homework and was traumatized grinding 12+ hours of heavy theory. Attendance is mandatory through an online iclicker. This course brought back some of my lost passion for math.
Really hard courses but explains the subjects to the best of his abilities.

I didn't think I would like 250 because I don't think of myself as a math person, but Prof. Golin made the class so interesting and engaging by how he related what we were learning to the real world. I did sit in the front and attend most classes, so I likely got more out of his lectures, but if you put decent effort into the class, it's rewarded.

Had him for both 250 and 210 --- respectable professor and teacher --- had a great experience. He helps visualize hard concepts and explains very well. Do your best to keep in the habit of paying attention because there's lots of useful information in class even if it's sometimes boring. I had a great year with him!
Really great professor.

not instructor specific, 250 this semester was taught together with barrington. both classes had the same HW and exams. lectures were also pretty similar, you get to attend either for attendence purposes. my advice overall is honestly just to go to SI sessions to learn the material and go to tutoring for the HW. the extra credit on HW does wonders.

Can vouch he is an amazing lecturer. He's very caring and happy to answer any questions. His care shines through his teaching and office hours. He always makes an effort to teach the material in a way that is more intuitive to students. Probably the best CS lecturer I have had at UMass.

Prof Mordecai is co-teaching using the same syllabus as Barrington, so it's unavoidable the class is still really hard just like it's always been. However, he's really caring, and I've pretty much seen him always willing to stay after class for like 30-45min outside the hallway to answer other students' questions.
I was a bit worried because this is his first semester at UMass, so there wasn't much information about him. However, after taking this class with him, I can vouch that he's an amazing lecturer!
Great lecturer

He is an amazing professor. I really enjoyed his lectures and I felt that he broke down material in a way that was palatable even for people that don't know much about computer systems. He is responsive on email and Ed. Class is overall very generously graded and he provided a lot of extra credit.
The sweetest professor I have had. That being said, his lectures simplified the course material too much, making it so that I had to self-teach a certain amount.

Nikko's lectures were fun and engaging, there were many opportunities for extra credit, and the "4 picks 2" policy made getting an A very comfortable and achievable. The textbook is wonderful and freely published online, the projects were all relevant and instructive, and the final was very fair. A great experience and I learned a lot.

Nikko is the best, if you have the opportunity to take his class, take it!!!!!!
I had Joe Chiu for 230 and then Nikko for 377. I wish I could go back and take 230 with him too. great guy

By far the most authentically caring and nice professor I've met. He made his lectures very fun and when I did go to class it was just for the good vibes. Not everyone (myself included) is a big fan of systems, but the labs and homework aren't toooo bad if your C is strong. The exams were very fair and I was able to self study pretty successfully.

The most genuine and caring professor ever. Nikko makes this course more interesting and fun by adding some animations and memes to the lecture slides. Really approachable and always care about our well-being. He makes the process of learning low-level programming a bit more enjoyable. If you're looking to take 230, definitely take with Nikko
Nikko is great, highly recommend taking 377 with him. He takes his time lecturing and makes sure you understand the material, and is a super nice guy.

I didn't enjoy this class very much but that was almost solely because of the course setup itself and Joe's structuring. Nikko is a very caring person, and a decent lecturer. He is helpful outside class and is clearly trying to improve. 100% take this course with him and NOT with Joe. Nikko's slides are better.

Nikko is an extremely personable and genuine guy. I liked how he incorporated memes into his slides and tried to dumb the content down, but I felt like the lectures were not really that engaging. The project documentation isn't great. The exams are generously graded. I don't like that the only extra credit relied on other ppl to answer survey.
Nikko is one of the nicest professors I have ever met, he's very genuine and cares about his students.

Nikko is one of the nicest professors at UMASS, he really does enjoy teaching and you can see that in his lectures. He responds very fast on Piazza and to emails and is very accommodating for any circumstances. I would recommend taking any course that Nikko teaches.
Nikko is an amazing professor! His class is very fair and he offers a ton of extra credit.
This guy is the most adorable guy on campus. Protect this man at all costs. The course content itself is very boring but he makes the class better with his presence.
Good Prof, Very Proactive, Helps out a lot, responds promptly. Projects are hard but going to office hours and attending lectures should do the trick.
Really sweet guy who tried to make classes as interactive as possible. Not a fan of lower-level programming, but he made the content enjoyable. Take it with him if you can!

Professor Nikko is an amazing teacher, he is really passionate about what he teaches and tries to make it fun for everyone and to break the concepts down. CS230 is hard but where he shines is the help outside of the class, he really tries to help every student who needs it. Very approachable and went above and beyond to make sure everyone passess.
One of the kindest and most approachable professors in the CS department. Nikko is awesome. This was his first time teaching this course, and he did a phenomenal job. Take him if you can!

Easily the nicest and most caring CS professor. No other CS professor is incorporating games into his lectures like Nikko. The HW's are very manageable and he makes the lecture content easy to understand. Truly a 5 star professor

Nikko was a great teacher who is very humble and down to earth. He is very engaging in lectures and clearly passionate about the field. He was also very responsive and helpful on the class forum for problems with homework and projects. The workload and grading are fair. Would recommend taking courses with him!

CICS 109 Intro to Data Analysis in R
INSTRUCTOR(S): Jasper McChesney
An introduction to data analysis in the open-source R language, with an emphasis on practical data work. Topics will include data
wrangling, summary statistics, modeling, and visualization. Will also cover fundamental programming concepts including data types,
functions, flow of control, and good programming practices. Intended for a broad range of students outside of computer science.
Some familiarity with statistics is expected. 1 credit.

CICS 110 Foundations of Programming
INSTRUCTOR(S): Kirat Arora, Evan Ciccarelli, Dung Viet Pham, Cole Reilly, Bryn Reimer, Ella Tuson
An introduction to computer programming and problem solving using computers. This course teaches you how real-world problems
can be solved computationally using programming constructs and data abstractions of a modern programming language. Concepts
and techniques covered include variables, expressions, data types, objects, branching, iteration, functions, classes, and methods.
We will also cover how to translate problems into a sequence of instructions, investigate the fundamental operation of a
computational system and trace program execution and memory, and learn how to test and debug programs. No previous
programming experience required. (Gen. Ed. R2) Prerequisite: R1 (or a score of 15 or higher on the math placement test Part A), or
one of the following courses: MATH 101&102 or MATH 104 or MATH 127 or MATH 128 or MATH 131 or MATH 132. 4 credits.

CICS 127 Intro to Public Interest Tech
INSTRUCTOR(S): Emily Nutwell
Today's world is complex and tech driven. How do we use the tools of information technology to solve problems in a socially
responsible way, i.e., in a way that both empowers us and promotes the well-being of the communities in which we live? In this
course, we describe the socio-technical world and pragmatic strategies for promoting personal and social responsibility. We explore
the questions: What is the public interest in a socio-technical world? What strategies can we use to promote social responsibility in
the public sector, private sector, and general public? What can each of us do to make the world a better place? This course is for
everyone at all levels and with all interests. No programming or prerequisites are required. We focus on building skills to think
analytically, broadly, and strategically, as well as to communicate effectively about complex problems with societal impact.
Assignments will provide students multiple paths to success. Counts towards the IT minor and is required for the PIT Undergraduate
Certificate. (Gen. Ed. SI) 4 credits.

CICS 160 Object-Oriented Programming
INSTRUCTOR(S): Neha Kennard, Cole Reilly, Ella Tuson
This course will expose students to programming practices beyond the introductory level, concentrating on Object Oriented
Programming techniques and an introduction to Data Structures. Students will also study and analyze the complexity of both the
algorithms presented in class and of the algorithms they develop. This course also provides experience with the development and
analysis of recursive algorithms and programs. Before taking this course, students are expected to have been exposed to the
following concepts through a college-level course or equivalent in some high level computer programming language: input and
output operations, conditional statements, loops, arrays, recursion, and functions/methods. The course places an emphasis on the
careful design and testing of programs. (Gen. Ed. R2) Prerequisite: CICS 110 (previously INFO 190S) or COMPSCI 121 with a grade
of C or better. 4 credits.

CICS 210 Data Structures
INSTRUCTOR(S): Mordecai Golin, Marc Liberatore
An introduction to the design, analysis, and implementation of data structures. This course teaches you how to build, test, debug,
document, and evaluate objects that encapsulate data and their associated operations using programming constructs and data
abstractions of a modern programming language. Concepts and techniques covered include linear and non-linear structures,
recursive structures and algorithms, traversal algorithms, binary search trees, balanced trees, priority queues, union-find, hash
tables, bloom filters, and graphs. We will also informally compare and contrast the run time efficiency of algorithms and their
performance characteristics including the concept of worst-case running time analysis and the classification of algorithms in terms of
constant,logarithmic,linear,loglinear,quadratic,andexponentialtimeusingBig-Onotation. (Gen.Ed.R2) Prerequisite:CICS160
(previously INFO 190T) with a grade of C or better. 4 credits.

CICS 256 Make: Physical Computing
INSTRUCTOR(S): Donghyun Kim
Inspired by the Maker movement, this course provides a hands-on introduction to physical computing: sensing and responding to
the physical world using computers. Specific topics include: basic electronics and circuit design, microcontroller programming using
Arduinos, sensing and responding to the physical world, rapid prototyping (3D printing and laser cutting etc.), soft circuits and
wearable electronics. The course will encourage and empower students to invent, design, and build practical hardware projects that
interact with the physical world. This course has a required lab section, and counts as one of the CS Lab Science Requirement
courses for the BS-CS. Prerequisite: CICS 210 (or COMPSCI 187) with a grade of C or better and completion of the R1 (Basic Math
Skills) Gen. Ed. 4 credits.

CICS 291C S-Find Strengths/Design Career
INSTRUCTOR(S): Casey Maloney
This course is designed to prepare CICS students for their internship and job searches, improve their professional skills (both
technical and soft) and help them approach professional development and/or advanced educational opportunities with confidence. 1
credit.
CICS 291T S-CICS Transfer Success
INSTRUCTOR(S): Casey Maloney
This seminar is intended to help you become fully prepared to succeed in CICS at UMass. Students in this seminar will be led by an
instructor with a detailed understanding of the transfer student experience, and supported by various staff members in CICS. You
will learn about which campus and College resources will be most helpful to you, how to best utilize these resources, and where you
can look for other opportunities to connect. 1 credit.

CICS 305 Social Issues in Computing
INSTRUCTOR(S): Erin Butler, Siobhan Meï, Justin Obara, Christina Sutcliffe
This class satisfies the Junior Year Writing requirement by providing instruction in several different technical communication genres,
including research writing and science writing. Writing contexts will include writing for municipal, public, and professional audiences.
Through our writing, we will engage with computing topics and challenges facing our current global context and explore various
impacts of computers on modern society. Students will produce approximately 20-25 pages of polished written work over the course
ofthesemester. CICSPrimaryMajorsonly. Prerequisite:CSMajors: ENGLWRIT112withagradeofCorbetter(orEnglishWriting
waiver), COMPSCI 220, COMPSCI 230 and COMPSCI 240 (or 250); INFORM Majors: ENGLWRIT 112 with a grade of C or better (or
English Writing waiver) and INFO 248. 3 credits.
CICS 590P TechPolicy & Innov/Common Good
INSTRUCTOR(S): Emily Nutwell, Carolina Rossini
In this course, we will introduce Public Interest Technology, innovation, and relevant regulatory frameworks to provide a foundation

In this course, we will introduce Public Interest Technology, innovation, and relevant regulatory frameworks to provide a foundation
toexplore thethoughtful design oftechnology thatcan serve society while remaining safe for allusers andstakeholders. Thecourse
begins with examining the core values of public interest technology, such as fairness, transparency, accountability, and inclusivity.
We will then review foundational concepts around innovation and strategies, along with an overview of technology governance. An
overview ofproduct development models, focusing onAgileproduct development willbepresented along withapresentation oftech
governance within an Agile framework. We will then analyze case studies where technologies have either succeeded or failed to
uphold these values, examining the societal impact of emerging technologies like artificial intelligence (AI), self-driving cars, digital
platforms, and data-driven systems. Throughout the semester, guest lectures from leaders in the fields of technology policy,
innovation, and governance will offer insights into how you can bridge the gap between regulatory goals and technological creativity

innovation, and governance will offer insights into how you can bridge the gap between regulatory goals and technological creativity
inyourfuturecareers.ThiscoursecountsasanElectivefortheCSandINFORMMajors. Cross-listedwithSPP590P. Undergraduate
Prerequisite: CICS 305 with a grade of C or better. 3 credits.

COMPSCI 119 Intro to Programming
INSTRUCTOR(S): Aline Weber
A complete introduction to computer programming using the Python language. Topics include coverage of all the supported data
types and program code structures, functions (up through lambda expressions and recursion), reasoning about and debugging
existing code, implementation of custom libraries, selection of data structures, and the fundamentals of object-oriented
programming. Students will create, debug, and run Python 3 programs that explore each of these topics in turn, from simple loops
up through the processing of large data sets, and eventually to the creation of professional-quality libraries to synthesize graphics
images and audio files. No prior programming experience expected. Not open to Computer Science majors. 3 credits.
COMPSCI 198C P-Intro/C Programming Language
INSTRUCTOR(S): Meng-Chieh Chiu, Timothy Richards
This practicum assumes general background and experience in computer programming (such as that provided by COMPSCI 121 or a
similar introductory programming course) and some knowledge of data structures. Content will include basic C data types,

similar introductory programming course) and some knowledge of data structures. Content will include basic C data types,
declarations, expressions, statements, and functions; simple use of macros; some common library calls (such as formatted
input/output); basic pointer manipulation using linked lists; and introduction to using standard tools (gcc and make). A required
prerequisite for COMPSCI 230, effective Fall 2023. Prerequisite: CICS 160 (previously INFO 190T or COMPSCI 186 or CICS 210)
with a grade of C or better, or COMPSCI 121 with a grade of B or better. 1 credit.

COMPSCI 220 Programming Methodology
INSTRUCTOR(S): Marius Minea, James Perretta
Development of individual skills necessary for designing, implementing, testing and modifying larger programs, including: design
strategies and patterns, using functional and object-oriented approaches, testing and program verification, code refactoring,
interfacing with libraries. There will be significant programming and mid-term and final examinations. Prerequisite: CICS 210 (or

COMPSCI 187) with a grade of C or better. 4 credits.
2026 Spring --- page 2 --- 1/15/2026

COMPSCI 230 Computer Systems Principles
INSTRUCTOR(S): Phuthipong Bovornkeeratiroj, Meng-Chieh Chiu
Large-scale software systems like Google - deployed over a world-wide network of hundreds of thousands of computers - have
become a part of our lives. These are systems success stories - they are reliable, available ("up" nearly all the time), handle an
unbelievable amount of load from users around the world, yet provide virtually instantaneous results. On the other hand, many
computer systems don't perform nearly as well as Google - hence the now-cliché "the system is down." In this class, we study the
scientific principles behind the construction of high-performance, scalable systems. The course begins with a discussion of C data
representation, and moves up the stack from there to the features of modern architectures, assembly languages, and operating
system services such as I/O, process, and synchronization. This class assumes students have either taken COMPSCI 198C or have
equivalent experience in the C programming language. Prerequisite: CICS 210 (or COMPSCI 187) with a grade of C or better and
COMPSCI 198C. 4 credits.

COMPSCI 240 Reasoning Under Uncertainty
INSTRUCTOR(S): Justin Domke, Ghazaleh Parvini
Development of mathematical reasoning skills for problems that involve uncertainty. Each concept will be illustrated by real-world
examples and demonstrated through in-class and homework exercises. Counting and probability -- basic counting problems,
probability definitions, mean, variance, binomial distribution, discrete random variables, continuous random variables, Markov and
Chebyshev bounds, Laws of large numbers, and central limit theorem. Probabilistic reasoning -- conditional probability and odds,
Bayes' Law, Markov Chains, Bayesian Networks. Statistical topics such as estimation of parameters and linear regression, as time
permits. Prerequisite: CICS 160 (previously INFO 190T or COMPSCI 187) or CICS 210 and MATH 132, all with a grade of C or
better. 4 credits.

COMPSCI 250 Introduction To Computation
INSTRUCTOR(S): David Barrington, Mordecai Golin
Basic concepts of discrete mathematics useful to computer science: set theory, strings and formal languages, propositional and

INSTRUCTOR(S): Matthew Rattigan
Data science uses various concepts, practices, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from data. It

integrated, these techniques can help human analysts make sense of vast stores of digital information. This course presents the

COMPSCI 377 Operating Systems
INSTRUCTOR(S): Phuthipong Bovornkeeratiroj
In this course we examine the important problems in operating system design and implementation. The operating system provides
a well-known, convenient, and efficient interface between user programs and the bare hardware of the computer on which they run.
The operating system is responsible for allowing resources (e.g., disks, networks, and processors) to be shared, providing common
services needed by many different programs (e.g., file service, the ability to start or stop processes, and access to the printer), and
protecting individual programs from one another. The course will start with a brief historical perspective of the evolution of
operating systems over the last fifty years, and then cover the major components of most operating systems. This discussion will
cover the tradeoffs that can be made between performance and functionality during the design and implementation of an operating
system. Particular emphasis will be given to three major OS subsystems: process management (processes, threads, CPU

system. Particular emphasis will be given to three major OS subsystems: process management (processes, threads, CPU
scheduling, synchronization, and deadlock), memory management (segmentation, paging, swapping), file systems, and operating
system support for distributed systems. Programming projects in C. This course counts as a CS Elective for the CS Major.
Prerequisite: COMPSCI 230 with a grade of C or better. 4 credits.

INSTRUCTOR(S): Adam O'Neill
This is an undergraduate-level introduction to cryptography. It is a theory course with a significant mathematical component.
However, our viewpoint will be “theory applied to practice” in that we will aim to treat topics in a way of applied value. We will
discuss cryptographic algorithms used in practice and how to reason about their security. More fundamentally, we will try to
understand what security “is” in a rigorous way that allows us to follow sound principles and uncover design weaknesses. The
primary topics are: blockciphers, pseudorandom functions, symmetric-key encryption schemes, hash functions, message
authentication codes, public-key encryption schemes, digital signature schemes, and public-key infrastructures. This course counts
as a CS Elective toward the CS Major. Prerequisite: COMPSCI 311 with a grade of C or better. 3 credits.

COMPSCI 590AB Quantum Cryptography
INSTRUCTOR(S): Filip Rozpedek
The ability to transmit quantum information over long distances will enable implementation of many fascinating quantum
communication tasks and provide us with novel capabilities that reach beyond what we can do over classical Internet alone.

COMPSCI 627 Fixing Social Media
INSTRUCTOR(S): Ethan Zuckerman
Over the past decade, user-generated participatory media – social media – has emerged as the dominant model for content of the
Internet. From Facebook to Twitter, YouTube to Wikipedia, content created by non-professionals and circulated for commercial and
non-commercial motives underpins seven of the top10 websites inthe US, and has become an increasingly important component of
the news ecosystem. While social media was initially hailed as a powerful tool for broadening civic participation, many problems
have emerged with the rise of the medium, from questions of whether social media usage is bad for our individual mental health, to
whether the fabric of our democracy is being damaged by disinformation, fragmentation and hyperpolarization. As legislators look to
regulate these platforms and commentators propose shutting them down entirely, this course looks for an alternative: affirmative
visionsofsocialmediathataregoodforindividualsandsociety,whichwecouldworktowardsbuilding. Thisclassexaminespossible

visionsofsocialmediathataregoodforindividualsandsociety,whichwecouldworktowardsbuilding. Thisclassexaminespossible
problems with existing modes of social media, discusses ways in which social media could be a benefit to individuals and societies,
develops case studies of successful and healthy online communities, and ultimately designs and builds tools to improve existing
social media systems or replace them with novel models. Students will write reflectively about weekly readings and discussions and
participate in multi-week projects, ultimately building teams to work on final projects. Meets with COMM 627 and SPP 627. 3
credits.

refinement. We will also cover core database implementation issues including storage and indexing, query processing and
optimization, and transaction management. Additionally, we will address challenges in modern information systems, including
scalability and parallelization, big data systems, cloud databases, dealing with graph and vector data, and the integration of
database management systems and machine learning. 3 credits.

Probabilisticgraphicalmodelsprovideanintuitivelanguagefordescribingthestructureofjointprobabilitydistributionsusinggraphs.
They enable the compact representation and manipulation of exponentially large probability distributions, which allows them to
efficiently manage the uncertainty and partial observability common in real-world problems. As a result, graphical models have
become invaluable tools in a wide range of areas from computer vision and sensor networks to natural language processing and
computational biology. The aim of this course is to develop the knowledge and skills necessary to effectively design, implement and
apply these models to solve real problems. The course will cover (a) Bayesian and Markov networks; (b) exact and approximate
inference methods; (c) estimation of the parameters and structure of graphical models; (d) broader topics in probabilistic inference
for statistics and machine learning. Students entering the class should have good programming skills and knowledge of algorithms.

COMPSCI H311 Intro to Algorithms (colloq)
INSTRUCTOR(S): Ghazaleh Parvini
The design and analysis of efficient algorithms for important computational problems. Emphasis on the relationships between
algorithms and data structures and on measures of algorithmic efficiency. Advanced graph algorithms, dynamic programming
applications,NP-completenessandspacecomplexity,approximationandrandomizedalgorithms.Experimentalanalysisofalgorithms
also emphasized. Use of computer required. Prerequisite: CICS 210 (or COMPSCI 187) and COMPSCI 250 (or MATH 455) all with a
grade of C or better (or COMPSCI 311 with a grade of B or better). 1 credit.

COMPSCI H466 Applied Cryptography colloq
INSTRUCTOR(S): Adam O'Neill
This colloquium will guide students in an in-depth study of both theoretical and practical cryptographic principles. Topics include
zero-knowledge proofs, derivatives of which are now used in anonymous cryptocurrency such as ZCash, secure messaging protocols
(e.g. Signal, Whatsapp), and secure multiparty computation. Other topics at student request. Prerequisite: COMPSCI 311 with a
grade of C or better (or COMPSCI 466 with a grade of B or better). 1 credit.

COMPSCI H589 Machine Learning (colloq)
INSTRUCTOR(S): Bruno Castro da Silva
This colloquium will enrich the primary course by focusing on reading, presenting, and discussing foundational and recent research
papers from the machine learning literature. Students will write weekly reading responses, and lead one to two group discussions
over the course of the semester. Prerequisite: Students must be enrolled in or have completed COMPSCI 589. 1 credit.

INFO 101 Introduction to Informatics
INSTRUCTOR(S): Michelle Trim
An introduction to the main concepts of Informatics. There are several 'Big Ideas' in computing, including but not limited to
abstraction, data and information, algorithms, programming, the internet, and the global impacts of computing. This class provides
an introduction to those ideas and considers some of the ways that those computing principles might be used to solve real world
problems. Computer-based assignments are an integral part of this course but no programming knowledge or prior programming
experience is expected or required. Open to undergraduate students NOT majoring in Computer Science. 3 credits.

INFO 150 Mathmtcl Fndtn for Informatics
INSTRUCTOR(S): Peter Haas
Mathematical techniques useful in the study of computing and information processing. The mathematical method of definition and
proof. Sets, functions, and relations. Combinatorics, probability and probabilistic reasoning. Graphs and trees as models of data
and of computational processes. Prerequisite: R1 math skills recommended. Not intended for Computer Science majors – students
interested in a majors-level treatment of this material should see COMPSCI 240 and 250 (or MATH 455). 3 credits.

INFO 203 A Networked World
INSTRUCTOR(S): Mohammadhassan Hajiesmaili
The course will cover the technical foundations of today’s communication networks, particularly the Internet. It will also address key
social, policy, economic and legal aspects of these networks, their use (and abuse), and their regulation. This course covers
computer science topics, but all material will be presented in a way that is accessible to an educated audience with or without a
strongtechnicalbackground. NotintendedforComputerSciencemajors–studentsinterestedinaCSmajors-leveltreatmentofthis
material should see COMPSCI 453. 3 credits.
2026 Spring --- page 16 --- 1/15/2026

INFO 248 Intro to Data Science
INSTRUCTOR(S): Gordon Anderson
Thiscourseisanintroductiontotheconceptsandskillsinvolvedwiththecollection,management,analysis,andpresentationofdata
sets and the data products that result from the work of data scientists. Privacy, algorithmic bias and ethical issues are also
discussed. Students will work with data from the financial, epidemiological, educational, and other domains. The course provides
examples of real-world data that students work with using various software tools. This course consists of two lecture meetings and
one lab meeting per week. Readings will be assigned as preparation for each class meeting. A semester project will be assigned.
Students work in pairs to develop their project over the semester. The project provides students with an opportunity to work
collaboratively to explore the topics in more depth in a specialized domain. A midterm and final exam will be given. Grades are
determined by a combination of scores on lab activities, projects, and exam scores. Software: all software is freely available. Open

determined by a combination of scores on lab activities, projects, and exam scores. Software: all software is freely available. Open
to INFORM majors. Prerequisite: a grade of C or or above in the following courses: CICS 110 (or CICS 160 or COMPSCI 119 or

COMPSCI 121) with a grade of C or above and either: PSYCH 240, OIM 240, STATISTC 240, RES-ECON 212, SOCIOL 212, OR
STATISTC 315/515, OR COMPSCI 240, with a grade of C or above. 4 credits.

INFO 348 Data Analytics with Python
INSTRUCTOR(S): Matthew Rattigan
The modern world is awash with data, and making sense of it requires specialized skills. This course will expose students to
commonly used data analytics techniques. Topics include the acquisition, manipulation, and transformation of structured data,
exploratory data analysis, data visualization, and predictive modeling. Students in this course will learn and use the Python
programming language and tools for working with data. Analysis will be performed using real data sets. Does not count as a CS
Elective (BA or BS). Satisfies one of the Data Science Concentration requirements and counts as an elective for the Health and Life
Sciences Concentration for the Informatics major. Open to INFORM majors. Prerequisite: INFO 248 and CICS 160 (or INFO 190T or

COMPSCI 186 or 187), both with a grade of C or better. 3 credits.
